EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and weak southeast flow resulted in thunderstorms that produced locally heavy rain and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Marana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water of over 1.5 inches along with upper level low pressure over Sonora and associated moderate southeast flow resulted in thunderstorms that produced damaging winds, mainly over western Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a power pole and damaged a roof of a residence near the Gunsight Cemetery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure sat over the Desert Southwest for two days, bringing heavy rain and snow to portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning knocked out power to 800 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure sat over the Desert Southwest for two days, bringing heavy rain and snow to portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding washed debris over the Willow Beach access road and around the campground and boat launch area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated trough of low pressure moved across California, the Gulf of California, and Arizona. A large area of light to moderate snow fell across northern Arizona. Two bands of heavy snow developed west and southeast of Flagstaff. These bands produced twice as much snowfall as other areas received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow developed over the western portions of the Western Mogollon Rim during the evening and persisted through the night. Fifteen inches of snow fell at the NWS office in Bellemont. Twelve inches of snow fell just outside of Parks. Arizona Snowbowl reported 8 to 12 inches (9,200 and 10,800 feet elevation).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated trough of low pressure moved across California, the Gulf of California, and Arizona. A large area of light to moderate snow fell across northern Arizona. Two bands of heavy snow developed west and southeast of Flagstaff. These bands produced twice as much snowfall as other areas received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow developed over western portions of the Eastern Mogollon Rim in the evening and persisted through the night. Ten inches of snow fell at the Happy Jack Ranger Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified arctic air mass moved over northern Arizona with several locations breaking low temperature records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grand Canyon Airport set a record low of -23F. The previous record of -15F was last set in 2009.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified arctic air mass moved over northern Arizona with several locations breaking low temperature records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bagdad tied the record low of 20F. The previous 20F was last seen in 1972.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought windy conditions to northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the St Johns Airport recorded a peak wind gust of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding in parts of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused urban street flooding with reports of water and mud on South Milton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm dumped heavy snow on the Kaibab Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 12 inches of snow fell in and around Jacob Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain Cataract and Supai Canyons causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A three foot rise on Havasu Creek was reported at 253 PM.  Later, at just after 400 PM, Supai Police reported that trails had been flooded and washed out.  One bridge was flooded and parts of the Village of Supai were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moist southerly flow fueled thunderstorms that produced heavy rain over the Yarnell Hill Fire scar. This triggered flash flooding in the town of Yarnell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Yarnell Hill Fire scar produced flash flooding in the town of Yarnell. Water was flowing over the roads near the junction of Willow and Stahl.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man fishing from a canoe on Big Lake was stuck and killed by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was stuck and killed by lightning while fishing from a canoe on Big Lake.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought breezy to windy conditions across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winslow ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified arctic air mass moved over northern Arizona with several locations breaking low temperature records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff Airport set a record low of -9F. The previous record of -8F was last set in 1951. Bellemont set a record low of -14F. The previous record of -9F was last set in 2009.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold upper level low pressure system developed across the western United States starting on Friday, January 11, and it persisted through Tuesday, January 15. The cold system led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across the central deserts, including the Greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Due to the freezing temperatures, a freeze warning was issued starting Friday, running through 9 am Tuesday morning. The freezing temperatures led to isolated occurrences of dangerous black ice across the greater Phoenix area on Tuesday morning; the ice was responsible for a significant traffic accident in central Phoenix. The cause of the water on the road was unknown, however it was not due to precipitation. Freezing temperatures caused the ice to freeze and to form the dangerous black ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold weather pattern over the western United States led to widespread freezing temperatures over the greater Phoenix area on Tuesday, January 15. There was water on the roadway in central Phoenix, and the freezing temperatures caused dangerous black ice to form. At 8 am, an SUV hit a patch of black ice and skidded off the road and into a Courtesy Chevrolet dealership located at 12th Street and Camelback road. The SUV slammed into a brand new Corvette and a Camaro, causing significant damage to both. The SUV then slammed into the dealership's showroom, causing major damage as well as a injury to someone in the showroom. All told, the damage estimates came to approximately 150,000 dollars. Damage to the Corvette alone was estimated to be 75,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved into eastern Arizona during the day on February 20th and produced locally heavy snowfall down to around 3000 feet across portions of southern Gila county. Several inches of new snow fell in the Globe and Miami areas from mid morning through early afternoon, and light amounts event fell down to 2500 feet or below in the foothills and higher desert elevations on the north and east fringes of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The snow led to the issuance of a Winter Storm Warning above 3500 feet for southern Gila county from 2 am until 8 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold, wet and slow moving Pacific low pressure system moved into eastern Arizona on February 20th, and it led to the development of locally heavy snow across portions of southern Gila and far northeastern Maricopa counties during the day. Snow levels fell as low as 2500 feet or so and locally heavy accumulations were noted near and above 3000 feet, affecting communities such as Globe, Miama and Superior. At about 1 pm, a trained weather spotter in Miami measured 4 inches of new snow. He also reported 2 inches of snow in Superior and indicated that highway 60 was closed between Superior and Miami due to slick and dangerous road conditions. Earlier in the day, at 1120 am, another trained spotter 1 mile northeast of Desert Mountain, estimated that 3 inches of new snow had fallen. Additionally, light amounts of snow were seen in the foothills and higher desert locations on the north and east desert fringes of the greater Phoenix area; the Diamondbacks spring training facility at Talking Stick Resort in Scottsdale was coated in a light layer of snow during the afternoon hours. The heavy snow led to the issuance of a Winter Storm Warning for southern Gila and northeast Maricopa counties between 2 am and 8 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and cold upper level low pressure center move southeast along the West Coast then eastward across Arizona. This brought areas of heavy snow to much of the area. Several weaker disturbances followed the main low with a continuation of snow showers with locally heavy accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell in Munds Park at an elevation of around 6500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and cold upper level low pressure center move southeast along the West Coast then eastward across Arizona. This brought areas of heavy snow to much of the area. Several weaker disturbances followed the main low with a continuation of snow showers with locally heavy accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Rimrock at an elevation of around 3600 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved east across the central Arizona deserts on the 8th of March, and the associated cold front passed over the area during the afternoon hours bringing gusty winds in excess of 50 mph, locally heavy rain and small to moderate hail. The cold front generated scattered thunderstorms, which produced locally damaging winds in the greater Phoenix area, and locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused some flooding of streets and washes. Flooding of washes occurred in the community of Scottsdale, and one woman was found dead in one of the flooded washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, some with moderate hail, moved across the greater Phoenix area during the middle of the afternoon on March 8th. Communities such as Ahwatukee and Guadalupe recorded hail approaching one inch in diameter. According to a National Weather Service employee, at 243 pm hail measured at three quarters of an inch in diameter fell near 38th street and Ray Road in Ahwatukee. Additionally, a trained weather spotter 1 mile east of Guadalupe reported nickel hail at the intersection of Kyrene and Guadalupe roads at 235 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system moving through the desert southwest on April 8th led to the development of strong, gusty and damaging southwest to west winds across the lower deserts of Arizona. The strong wind led to the issuance of a variety of products during the afternoon hours, including Wind Advisories and Blowing Dust Advisories. Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were common and were sufficiently strong to cause isolated damage to trees and weaker structures in local communities such as Yuma. Wind damage was not a result of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm moved across the desert southwest during the day on April 8th, and it led to strong, gusty and damaging southwest to west winds across the southwest Arizona deserts. A Wind Advisory was issued for Yuma county beginning at 10 AM and it continued through 8 pm. According to a trained weather spotter, at 5 pm a large ficus tree was blown over and fell onto a home at 19th Street and McKinley in Yuma. Wind gusts were estimated to be near 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved east across the central Arizona deserts on the 8th of March, and the associated cold front passed over the area during the afternoon hours bringing gusty winds in excess of 50 mph, locally heavy rain and small to moderate hail. The cold front generated scattered thunderstorms, which produced locally damaging winds in the greater Phoenix area, and locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused some flooding of streets and washes. Flooding of washes occurred in the community of Scottsdale, and one woman was found dead in one of the flooded washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a passing cold fornt moved east and across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on March 8th. Some of the storms produced damaging outflow winds in excess of 60 mph. Some of the storms affected east valley communties such as Mesa. At 239 pm, a large tree was blown down at the Mesa Riverview shopping center, according to a public report. No damage or injuries were reported due to the downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved east across the central Arizona deserts on the 8th of March, and the associated cold front passed over the area during the afternoon hours bringing gusty winds in excess of 50 mph, locally heavy rain and small to moderate hail. The cold front generated scattered thunderstorms, which produced locally damaging winds in the greater Phoenix area, and locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused some flooding of streets and washes. Flooding of washes occurred in the community of Scottsdale, and one woman was found dead in one of the flooded washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, associated with a passing cold front, moved east across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on March 8th. Some of the storms generated gusty and damaging outflow winds. According to a public report, at 243 pm a number of tree limbs were blown down at 60th street between Indian School and Thomas roads in downtown Scottsdale. No injuries were reported. Wind gusts were estimated to be at least 50 mph, and the time of damage was estimated based on radar information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved east across the central Arizona deserts on the 8th of March, and the associated cold front passed over the area during the afternoon hours bringing gusty winds in excess of 50 mph, locally heavy rain and small to moderate hail. The cold front generated scattered thunderstorms, which produced locally damaging winds in the greater Phoenix area, and locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused some flooding of streets and washes. Flooding of washes occurred in the community of Scottsdale, and one woman was found dead in one of the flooded washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on March 8th over portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The storms generated locally heavy rainfall; radar indicated that rainfall totals exceeded one inch with some of the stronger storms. Although Flash Flood Warnings were not issued, an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for south central Arizona at 120 pm. According to a National Weather Service employee, the locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused flooding of highway 238 west of Maricopa during the early afternoon hours. At 215 pm, water was reported to be one foot deep on highway 238 approximately 3 miles east northeast of Estrella. The highway was impassable by car. Fortunately, no accidents of injuries were reported due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved east across the central Arizona deserts on the 8th of March, and the associated cold front passed over the area during the afternoon hours bringing gusty winds in excess of 50 mph, locally heavy rain and small to moderate hail. The cold front generated scattered thunderstorms, which produced locally damaging winds in the greater Phoenix area, and locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused some flooding of streets and washes. Flooding of washes occurred in the community of Scottsdale, and one woman was found dead in one of the flooded washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a powerful winter storm dropped locally heavy rainfall across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, including the community of Scottsdale, during the afternoon hours on March 8th. An Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued at 120 pm for south central Arizona, including Fountain Hills and Scottdale, and it continued through 315 pm. Radar indicated that rain in excess of 1 inch fell during this period, with additional rain occurring late into the day. The rain led to the flooding of washes in Scottsdale. According to a Fox 10 article, Scottsdale police identified the body of a woman found in a rain swollen wash in Scottsdale on Saturday afternoon on March 9th. The woman, 38 years old, was found in the wash just north of Chaparral Road, off Hayden Road. She lived in a group home about 1 imile away from the wash and she was reported missing Friday as the weather worsened. A man who was in the area Saturday afternoon spotted the woman floating in the water, and called 911. Police then arrived at the wash at about 430 pm Saturday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system moving through the desert southwest on April 8th led to the development of strong, gusty and damaging southwest to west winds across portions of south central Arizona, including southern Gila county and the greater Phoenix area. The strong wind led to the issuance of a variety of products during the afternoon hours, including Wind Advisories and Blowing Dust Advisories. Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were common and were sufficiently strong to cause isolated damage to trees and weaker structures in local communities such as Globe. Wind damage was not a result of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm moved slowly east across Arizona on April 8th, and it led to strong and gusty southwest winds during the afternoon hours across much of south central Arizona, including the community of Globe. The strong winds led to the issuance of a Wind Advisory at 10 am, which continued into the early evening hours. A trained weather spotter 1 mile east of Globe measured a wind gust of 60 mph at 1214 pm, and the gust was not associated with a thunderstorm. No damages were reported due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over the Four Corners area and resultant southeast flow kept abundant moisture between 1.5 and 2 inches of precipitable water over southeast Arizona. A disturbance triggered northeast moving thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and ponding of water in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 1 to 2 inches of rain in less than an hour over parts of Eloy. Without sufficient drainage, water quickly ponded, flooding streets and yards.  Water was a foot deep in some streets, stranding several vehicles.   Damage due to flooding of homes was minimal, but extensive areas of mud were left on streets and in yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a northeast upper level flow continued, low level moisture increased aboard lower level southeast winds. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding in parts of the Tucson metro area and locations just south and east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Ocotillo Road northwest of Benson to become flooded. A motorist was rescued from a vehicle in flowing water 2 feet deep. Roads were also flooded in the Mescal area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture in easterly airflow led to isolated thunderstorms, mainly east of Tucson. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and blowing dust in the Douglas area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds blew roofs off several buildings at the state prison near the Bisbee-Douglas International Airport. One outbuilding was blown into a van, causing damage to the vehicle. Several power poles were downed causing a power outage which lasted for seven hours.  The ASOS at KDUG measured a gust to 58 mph before power was lost.  The strong winds also generated areas of blowing dust which briefly restricted visibility to near one half of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a wildfire in the Gila River bottom southeast of Kearny on July 8th.  The Shipman Fire consumed 518 acres before being fully contained on July 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shipman Fire began when a lightning strike sparked a fire in the Gila River bed which was thick with salt cedar trees just southeast of Kearny.  About 200 homes were evacuated and Highway 177 was closed for a short time as a precaution.  One home and two other small structures were destroyed by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved across Chihuahua and Sonora helping to strengthen thunderstorms near the international border. Abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water led to thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and flash flooding over southern Cochise County. A few storms also produced damaging winds and large hail as they moved northeast to southwest. The hardest hit area was from Sierra Vista to Palominas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail as it tracked westward across rural portions of southern Santa Cruz County. The largest hail reported by spotters was the size of golf balls at Kino Springs. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved across Chihuahua and Sonora helping to strengthen thunderstorms near the international border. Abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water led to thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and flash flooding over southern Cochise County. A few storms also produced damaging winds and large hail as they moved northeast to southwest. The hardest hit area was from Sierra Vista to Palominas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked over irrigation sprinklers, destroyed a barn, tore roofs off several other buildings, including a business in the Pearce-Sunsites area. The damaging winds also downed trees and power poles and blew a semi-tractor trailer onto its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved across Chihuahua and Sonora helping to strengthen thunderstorms near the international border. Abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water led to thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and flash flooding over southern Cochise County. A few storms also produced damaging winds and large hail as they moved northeast to southwest. The hardest hit area was from Sierra Vista to Palominas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle windshield was broken by large hail near Kings Ranch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved across Chihuahua and Sonora helping to strengthen thunderstorms near the international border. Abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water led to thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and flash flooding over southern Cochise County. A few storms also produced damaging winds and large hail as they moved northeast to southwest. The hardest hit area was from Sierra Vista to Palominas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 58 mph was measured at the Nogales International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area of extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted across southeast Arizona during June. Modest rain fell on the western perimeter of this area in mid June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted across the southern halves of Graham and Greenlee Counties and the northeast two thirds of Cochise County in June. These dry conditions were exacerbated by very hot temperatures throughout the month. While modest rain fell over western parts of this drought area on the 15th and the 30th, little if any rain occurred elsewhere.   The effects of prolonged drought were manifested when the Gila River in Graham County ran dry for the first time in a number of years. Farmers have been relegated to using what little well water remains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the Great Basin resulted in moderate northeast flow over southeast Arizona.  With surface dew points in the 30s and 40s, thunderstorms produced gusty winds. Due to recent dry conditions, those winds created areas of blowing dust.  In addition, lightning strikes created at least three separate fires, including one in the Santa Catalina Mountains known as the Prison Wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced wind gusts of 58 to 68 mph across southern sections of Tucson. While no damage was reported, blowing dust reduced visibilities to less than a quarter mile in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure from Utah to New Mexico resulted in moist southeast flow across southeast Arizona. An upper air disturbance triggered thunderstorms over Cochise, Graham and Greenlee Counties, some of which produced damaging winds or heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly three inches of rain fell in less than an hour over the upper reaches of Bonita Canyon within the burn scar of the 2011 Horseshoe 2 Wildfire. Significant mud and debris flow, rock slides, downed trees and flood damage to the Bonita Canyon Drive roadbed and Bonita Canyon Campground occurred in the upper reaches of the canyon.  Up to 18 inches of water and mud damaged the historic campground restroom facilities . Downstream, the visitor center parking lot was also flooded. The lower portion of the roadway reopened the next day while upper reaches remained closed for several weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Doce Fire (pronounced Dough-see) was detected at 1130 AM The Doce Fire approximately 8 miles northwest of Prescott, AZ near Granite Mountain Recreation Area and Dosie Pit Road. It was human caused. The fire was 100% contained on July 12 at 1800 at 6,767 acres. Most of that burned in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Doce Fire started in June and was 100% contained on July 12 at 1800 at 6,767 acres.  Total cost to fire the fire was around $7.4 million with $6.9 million spent in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Yarnell Hill Fire started around 536 PM by lightning on Friday June 28th, 2013 about 3.5 miles west of Yarnell, Arizona. The average daily temperature was above average for this area from June 26-July 10...and more than 10 degrees above average for June 28-July 1. The fire grew to about 8,300 acres by the end of June and a total of 8,400 acres before it was fully contained on July 10 around 600 PM. The Yavapai County Sheriff's Office said that 127 buildings in Yarnell and two in Peeples Valley were destroyed in the fire. On Sunday June 30, 2013 around 547 PM, strong winds from a thunderstorm to the northeast pushed the fire over 19 Hot Shots. They all perished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yarnell Hill Fire was started by lightning around 536 PM on Friday June 28 about 3.5 miles west of Yarnell. The fire grew to about 6 acres by Saturday morning. Strong outflow winds form a thunderstorm northeast of Prescott hit the fire Sunday afternoon. The wind caused the fire to blow up and it overtook 19 Granite Mountain Hot Shots. They all perished. The fire grew to around 8,300 acres by the end of June (the final size was 8,400 acres). The Yavapai County Sheriff's Office said that 127 buildings in Yarnell and two in Peeples Valley were destroyed in the fire. ||Officials shut down 25 miles of Arizona State Route 89 shortly after the fire started. All of Yarnell was evacuated and part of Peeples Valley was evacuated (at least 600 people total). The Red Cross said that 351 people spent at least one night at one of their shelters. ||A Type 2 incident management team was in charge of the fire late on June 30, with a Type 1 team ordered. Southwest Area Type 1 IMT assumed management of the fire on July 1. ||Resources on June 30 included 16 engines, eight water tenders, two crash-rescue vehicles, two structure protection vehicles, one bulldozer, one hotshot crew (with another four on order), seven Type 2 handcrews, a camp crew, and four Type 2 hand crews on order. One very large airtanker (VLAT) and an air attack unit were also on order.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon rainfall helped shrink the area of extreme drought (D3) conditions in southeast Arizona toward the end of July. Portions of Graham and Greenlee counties remained dry as the bulk of the rainfall occurred in Cochise County, especially near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought eased markedly across Cochise County during July as monsoon rainfall of mounts of 3 to 10 inches, representing 125 to 250 percent of normal, fell across the region. By July 23rd, all extreme drought (D3) was removed from Cochise county. The northern third of the county improved to the severe drought (D2) category while the remainder of Cochise County, where the heaviest rain fell, improved to either the D0 or D1 drought category. The Bisbee-Douglas International Airport recorded 10.12 inches of rain, the most ever measured there in one calendar month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved across Chihuahua and Sonora helping to strengthen thunderstorms near the international border. Abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water led to thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and flash flooding over southern Cochise County. A few storms also produced damaging winds and large hail as they moved northeast to southwest. The hardest hit area was from Sierra Vista to Palominas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to five inches of rain fell along Highway 92 between Sierra Vista and Bisbee.  Some areas received up to 7 inches of rain over a 3 hour period.  Vehicles became stranded on roadways in water up to 3 feet deep, necessitating several swift water rescues. Several roads were closed, including Highway 92, which was shut down for about 90 minutes.  Also, a large concrete culvert under a private road collapsed near Palominas.  Several residents were isolated due to the collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Doce Fire (pronounced Dough-see) was detected at 1130 AM The Doce Fire approximately 8 miles northwest of Prescott, AZ near Granite Mountain Recreation Area and Dosie Pit Road. It was human caused. The fire was 100% contained on July 12 at 1800. The wildfire burned 6,767 acres total of the Prescott National Forest, mostly in the Granite Mountain Wilderness. Most of the fire spread was in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Doce Fire (pronounced Dough-see) was reported at 1130 AM on June 18. Dry and breezy to windy conditions lasted from June 18 through June 24.  This pushed the fire to the northeast over the Granite Mountains.  The fire grew to 6,767 by June 24.  During the peak staffing there were 650 personnel including: 16 hand crews, 22 engines, 5 water tenders, 2 dozers, and 4 helicopters.  The firefighting cost was $6.9 million by the end of June. Only one minor injury was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree near 29th Street and Houghton Road and downed power lines at Irvington and Houghton Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over southeast Arizona coupled with adequate moisture resulted in thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and damaging winds.  Wind damage was most extensive in the Rita Ranch area of Tucson and in Green Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees including three 45-year old eucalyptus trees. Some trees fell onto houses causing minor roof damage and one ruptured a roof-mounted gas line. Another downed tree shattered a patio wall. Several 16 foot garage doors on newer homes were damaged or destroyed because the side of garage was constructed with an opening to a gated patio between the house and attached garage. The fire station at Esperanza Blvd. and La Canada Drive also sustained minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over southeast Arizona coupled with adequate moisture resulted in thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and damaging winds.  Wind damage was most extensive in the Rita Ranch area of Tucson and in Green Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in Rita Ranch.  One tree crashed into the living room of a home, while another smashed a cinder block wall and yet another fell onto a truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over southeast Arizona but enough moisture in the mid levels and with very warm temperatures at the surface combined to lead to a few thunderstorms that produced damaging winds.

Four homes were damaged, a pump house was destroyed, a center pivot irrigation system was flipped and the trunk of a 40-foot high tree was snapped near the ground. A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 78 mph and one member of the public reported winds in excess of 68 mph for fifteen minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and a moderate easterly mid level flow produced thunderstorms with damaging winds, large hail and heavy rain across parts of southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain for the second evening in a row caused flash flooding near Highway 92 from Palominas to near Nicksville and the foothills of the Huachuca Mountains. Several roads became inundated with flowing water two feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong impulse in northerly flow enhanced thunderstorms over southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms produced strong winds and blowing dust.  The most significant blowing dust occurred near the Willcox Playa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed large tree limbs near Benson.  Also, a field of planted rye was mostly flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area of extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted across southeast Arizona during June. Modest rain fell on the western perimeter of this area in mid June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted across the southern halves of Graham and Greenlee Counties and the northeast two thirds of Cochise County in June. These dry conditions were exacerbated by very hot temperatures throughout the month. While modest rain fell over western parts of this drought area on the 15th and the 30th, little if any rain occurred elsewhere. The effects of prolonged drought were manifested when the Gila River in Graham County ran dry for the first time in a number of years. Farmers have been relegated to using what little well water remains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure that had been centered over Arizona began to move northwest on July 1st. This caused moisture to move over the state from the east. Strong thunderstorms developed as a result of this moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy Rain Fell across Flagstaff. 1.10 inches of rain fell at a NWS employee's house.  Street flooding was reported and several intersections had water one to two feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of far southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours on August 22nd, and they affected the community of Yuma, as well as surrounding communities such as Fortuna Foothills and Somerton. The thunderstorms were quite intense and produced a wide variety of weather, including strong damaging winds in excess of 50 mph, locally dense blowing dust, moderate hail and locally heavy rain. Winds were sufficient to blow down power lines and blow over trailer trucks. Hail nearly one inch in diameter fell in Yuma, and rainfall in the Fortuna Foothills approached 2 inches, leading to localized flooding. A wide variety of products were issued including Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of far southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours on August 22nd and they affected the communities of Yuma and the Fortuna Foothills. In addition to producing gusty and damaging winds, they also produced locally heavy rainfall with rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. A trained weather spotter 2 miles southeast of the Fortuna Foothills measured rainfall at a rate of 1.89 inches per hour. The heavy rain caused localized flooding in the area around the Fortuna Foothills and Yuma, and led to the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory starting at 1720MST and continuing past 1915MST. In addition to the spotter report, radar indicated that up to 2 inches of rain fell from 1700MST through 1900MST in the Yuma area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon rainfall helped shrink the area of extreme drought (D3) conditions in southeast Arizona toward the end of July. Portions of Graham and Greenlee counties remained dry as the bulk of the rainfall occurred in Cochise County, especially near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area under extreme drought (D3) conditions shrank only slightly in southern Graham and Greenlee counties as the bulk of monsoon rain fell both to the north and south of the Gila River Valley. Most locations received between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rain during July, which is near or slightly below normal. The Safford airport received just 1.49 inches of rain.  Some of the effects on the cotton crop have been mitigated by farmers leaving half of their fields idle this season.  Mandatory water restrictions adopted by the City of Safford in August of 2012 have helped mitigate the city's supply to some extent, but due to the prolonged drought, the low water table caused the city to shut off four of the city's eleven wells. This in turn prompted the city to approve higher water rates to fund the drilling of new wells and building of pipelines. In nearby Thatcher, mandatory water restrictions were ordered and stricter water conservation standards have been adopted for new construction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow spread copious amounts of tropical moisture northward and into the south central Arizona deserts during the morning hours on September 9th. Widespread showers with moderate to heavy rain developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and this led to episodes of flash flooding and urban flooding which started in the late morning hours. In addition to the showers, there were embedded thunderstorms which served to locally enhance rainfall totals. Local storm spotters along with Flood Control District gage reports indicated that between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell during the day. Numerous reports of flash flooding and urban flooding were received from across the greater Phoenix area, starting in the late morning and continuing into the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow imported copious amounts of tropical moisture into south central Arizona during the morning hours on September 9th, and as a result widespread showers developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Areas of moderate to heavy rain developed, isolated embedded thunderstorms further enhanced the rainfall totals. Flood Control District gages and radar data indicated that rainfall rates between one and two inches per hour had occurred during the late morning and this led to flash flooding. According to a public reports, at 1145MST there was water flowing across the road at Cave Creek Road and Rose Garden Lane, about 3 miles east of the Deer Valley airport. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area beginning at 1133MST and continuing through 1300MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Diamond Bar Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three cars were stuck in water, with water lapping over the hoods. Water and debris flowed over several roads, and one swift water rescue took place. Three rain gages in the area measured 2 to 2.5 inches of rain in two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure brought record-breaking temperatures to much of the Desert Southwest. Several people died from the heat, many more were injured, and some infrastructure was damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached each day from the 1st through the 4th.  On the 1st, one person was injured and one dog died from the heat in Oatman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over Mohave County overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds tore an awning off a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over Mohave County overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the early afternoon hours on June 12th. The storms produced damaging winds in excess of 60 mph which affected portions of the greater Phoenix area as well as communities across northwest Pinal county. In addition, the gusty winds generated dense blowing dust, sufficient to produce dust storm conditions across the central deserts. Dust storm conditions were reported by a number of trained weather spotters in the greater Phoenix area during the early afternoon hours on June 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours across portions of north central Pinal county on July 12th. The storms produced gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph, which affected communities such as San Tan Valley and Florence. At 1230 pm, a trained weather spotter 2 miles north of Florence reported that a large tree had been blown over in Florence Gardens. In addition, an awning had been blown off of a house in the same area. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning had been issued for the area beginning at 1224 pm and continuing through 115 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms, with locally heavy rain, developed over the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 15th. Some of the storms produced gusty and damaging winds, estimated to reach or exceed 60 mph. The intensity of the storms warranted the issuance of several products such as Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban Flood Advisories. The gusty winds blew down a large number of trees in the greater Phoenix area, and even blew a semi trailer-truck off of Interstate 10 near Wild Horse Pass road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the greater Phoenix area during the late afternoon hours on July 15th, and they affected the community of Ahwatukee. The storms produced gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph. According to a trained weather spotter, at 535 pm over 50 trees were blown down in the Lakewood subdivision. Some fallen trees caused other damage to cars and property. The tree diameters were all 12 inches or larger. In addition, there was roof damage noted on a concrete tile roof. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning had been issued for the area starting at 454 pm and continuing until 545 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area of extreme drought (D3) conditions in southeast Arizona remained unchanged throughout August, essentially encompassing the southern halves of Graham and Greenlee counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area under extreme drought (D3) conditions remained unchanged as most locations in southern Graham and Greenlee counties received between 0.5 and 1.5 inches of rainfall during August, or about half of normal. The Safford airport received just 1.3 inches of rain. The Pinale��o Mountains were the exception to the dry trend where rainfall was near or even above normal. Some of the effects on the cotton crop have been mitigated by farmers leaving half of their fields idle this season. Mandatory water restrictions adopted by the City of Safford in August of 2012 have helped mitigate the city's supply to some extent, but due to the prolonged drought, the low water table caused the city to shut off four of the city's eleven wells. This in turn prompted the city to approve higher water rates to fund the drilling of new wells and building of pipelines. The estimated cost of a mile of new piping from proposed new wells south of the city was over $1 million. In nearby Thatcher, mandatory water restrictions were ordered and stricter water conservation standards have been adopted for new construction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs 2-4 inches in diameter were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over Mohave County overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped seven power poles on Route 66 near Oatman, knocking out power to the entire town for 18 hours.  Later in the afternoon, a man was treated for heat-related injuries, and one dog died from the heat.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general store in Oatman was inundated by flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tour bus drove into a flooded wash at mile marker 16 on Pierce Ferry Road, floated down the wash, and lodged on its side on an embankment. Everyone was rescued. The bus was a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the south central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on July 19th. In addition, very heavy rain occurred and some locations reported amounts up to 1.5 inches in less than one hour. According to a trained weather spotter several miles northwest of Deer Valley Airport, at 1012 pm gusty winds blew down several Palo Verde and Mesquite trees. A chain-link fence was also blown over. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the area at the time; it started at 1001 pm and continued through 1030 pm.

They affected the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, including the community of Queen Creek. The storms generated strong and damaging winds. According to local broadcast media, at about 1 pm, thunderstorm winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph blew over a 7 foot high steel wall. The wall fell on top of an person, causing injuries. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the area beginning at 1243 pm and continuing until 145 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central Street in Colorado City was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across portions of the central Arizona deserts, and they produced strong and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 50 mph. Some of the communities affected included Casa Grande and Coolidge. Thunderstorm wind damage included power poles that were blown over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening hours on July 6th and they affected portions of the central deserts including northern Pinal county. The storms generated strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds estimated to be in excess of 50 mph. According to local broadcast media, at 955 pm gusty winds blew down several trees about 4 miles east northeast of San Tan Valley. In addition, the winds blew over an RV causing damage, destoryed/damaged a small sheet metal building, blew off sheet metal of a few buildings, and caused shingle damage to a home. No injuries were reported. Although a Severe Thunderstorm Warning was not in effect at the time, a Significant Weather Advisory had been posted at 944 pm highlighting the potential for strong and gusty thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours across southern Gila county on July 10th. The storms generated strong and gusty winds, and also moderate to large hail. No damage was reported due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across southern Gila County during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 10th. Some storms affected the area around Hayden and Winkelman. At 603 pm, a public report was received of large hail in the town of Winkelman. The hail was estimated to be 0.75 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the deserts of south central Arizona during the late afternoon hours on August 26th, and they affected Casa Grande as well as the surrounding areas. The atmosphere was moist and very unstable and this allowed the thunderstorms to produce strong and damaging winds. A trained weather spotter about 2 miles northeast of Casa Grande reported debris, including a basketball hoop, flying down the street due to very strong winds. Another trained spotter on the northeast side of Casa Grande reported damage at a Home Depot. Cars in the parking lot were damaged by heavy debris blown about by winds of at least 60 mph. Additionally, a co-op observer 4 miles northwest of Eloy reported that several trees were downed by strong winds. He also estimated wind gusts to be around 60 mph. Two Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued for these damaging wind events; the first was issued at 1726MST and expired at 1745MST. The second was issued at 1747MST and ran through 1845MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms moved across the southeast portions of the greater Phoenix area on August 26th, and they impacted the community of Tempe. Thunderstorm outflow winds estimated to be at least 60 mph downed a large tree at an apartment complex located at the intersection of Kyrene and Elliot roads in Tempe. The tree landed on top of 3 adjacent parked cars and caved in the roofs of each car. The report was received via local broadcast media.

In addition, very heavy rain occurred and some locations reported amounts up to 1.5 inches in less than one hour. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across the northern portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the evening hours on July 19th. The storms generated gusty winds estimated to reach or exceed 65 mph. According to a trained weather spotter, at 1020 pm near 25th Avenue and West Happy Valley Road, 25 foot long tree branches were blown down. Winds estimated to be at least 50 mph were strong enough to shake vehicles in the area. In addition, another trained spotter 5 miles north of central Phoenix reported a large tree uprooted at 1020 pm by winds estimated to be at least 65 mph. The diameter of the trunk was estimated at 15 inches. The uprooted tree was located at the intersection of 16th Street and Greenway Parkway. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the area; it was issued at 1001 pm and continued until 1030 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours across portions of north central Pinal county on June 12th. The storms produced gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph, which affected communities such as San Tan Valley and Florence. At 1240 pm, a member of the public reported an 8 inch diameter tree blown down 1 mile east of San Tan Valley. At 1249 pm, a trained weather spotter 2 miles east of San Tan Valley reported several 6 to 8 inch diameter trees blown down. He estimated winds to be upwards of 60 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm coverage increased a little over the Mojave Desert as dry air began to push in above low level monsoon moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water entered two homes in Colorado City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms moved across portions of the south central deserts, including northwestern Maricopa county and eastern La Paz county, during the morning hours on September 10th.  The atmosphere remained very moist and rather unstable and locally heavy rainfall occurred which led to episodes of flash flooding and flooding which continued into the afternoon. Heavy rainfall that had occurred the previous day exacerbated the flooding problems. Many washes were running heavily including the Centennial Wash near Wenden, as well as local washes across northwestern Maricopa county north of Interstate 10. Other areas affected included the Grand Avenue corridor from Wittmann and Circle City northwest towards Wickenburg. Both Flash Flood Warnings and Areal Flood Warnings were issued due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late morning of September 10th thunderstorms developed from north of Wickenburg southward towards the northwestern fringes of the greater Phoenix area. They produced locally heavy rainfall; a flood control gage just east of Wickenburg measured 1.25 inches of rain within 15 minutes ending at 1134MST and another gage in Circle City measured 1.26 inches in less than one hour. Flash flooding resulted across portions of north central Maricopa county and by 1125MST a Flash Flood Warning was in effect. Radar and gage data indicated that rainfall rates approached 2 inches per hour in the area from Circle City northwestward towards Wickenburg; at 1233MST a Maricopa County Sheriff's deputy reported flash flooding in the Community of Circle City.  Heavy rains continued into the early afternoon and then gradually tapered off. Due to the persistent runoff impacting area roads as well as heavily running washes, an Areal Flood Warning was then issued at 1330MST for the Grand Avenue Corridor extending from Wittmann to Wickenburg and it continued until 2130MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the early afternoon hours on June 12th. The storms produced damaging winds in excess of 60 mph which affected portions of the greater Phoenix area as well as communities across northwest Pinal county. In addition, the gusty winds generated dense blowing dust, sufficient to produce dust storm conditions across the central deserts. Dust storm conditions were reported by a number of trained weather spotters in the greater Phoenix area during the early afternoon hours on June 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across northern Pinal county during the early afternoon hours on July 12th, and they produced gusty winds in excess of 50 mph which spread to the north and northwest and into the greater Phoenix area. As the winds spread out they picked up substantial amounts of dust and produced dust storm conditions, with visibilities lowering to near zero miles at times. Beginning at about 115 pm, and continuing through 2 pm, at least half a dozen trained weather spotters across the eastern and southeast portions of the greater Phoenix area reported dust storm conditions. At 116 pm a trained spotter at Superstition Springs Mall reported a quarter mile visibility at Power Road and US 60. At 120 pm, another spotter 3 miles northeast of Gilbert reported a quarter mile visibility and 40 mph winds. At 140 pm, a trained spotter 1 mile southwest of Fountain Hills reported 45 mph winds and visibility lowered below one quarter mile in dense blowing dust. Fortunately, no serious accidents were reported due to the dense blowing dust. A Dust Storm Warning was issued for the greater Phoenix area, beginning at 111 pm and continuing until 3 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours to the south of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, and they generated gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph. The strong winds spread to the north and across portions of northwest Pinal county, stirring up copious amounts of dust and creating dust storm conditions. A trained weather spotter 8 miles southwest of the town of Maricopa reported visibility less than one quarter of a mile at 1729MST. Another weather spotter 1 mile southwest of Coolidge reported a similar visibility. The dense blowing dust made for very hazardous driving conditions during the Monday rush hour. A Dust Storm Warning was issued for the area at 1712MST and it continued through 1900MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with heavy rain hit the east side of Flagstaff, Mt Elden, and Doney Park. Flood water and debris caused partial and full road closures in that area. Several campers were trapped in a campground until the dirt road could be bull dozed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the east side of Mt Elden and part of the Schultz Fire scar lead to water and debris flowing over residential roads and Highway 89.  Water entered several trailers and knocked down a slump block wall around the trailer park. Road crews had to clear the debris off the roads. Law enforcement reported widespread flooding from the Flagstaff Mall to Silver Saddle Road/Highway 89.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered late afternoon thunderstorms moved across the southeast portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area on August 26th, and they affected the community of Gilbert. The atmosphere was moist and quite unstable and this allowed the thunderstorms to produce strong and gusty winds, estimated to be at least 60 mph. A trained weather spotter 2 miles southeast of Gilbert reported that a large tree was downed in far southern Gilbert, near the Chandler Heights and Higley area. He also indicated that large tree branches were broken in the same area. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the Gilbert area at 1821MST and it continued until 1845MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms affected the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on August 26th, and they affected the towns of Buckeye, Goodyear and Litchfield Park. The Buckeye airport mesonet sensor measured a sustained wind at 43 mph as well as a peak gust of 68 mph. In addition, a trained weather spotter located 2 miles northeast of Goodyear reported trees uprooted by the strong winds. According to a report from local broadcast media, several trees were uprooted near Litchfield park. Finally, another broadcast media report indicated that a large tree was blown down. When the tree fell it heavily damaged a home in Litchfield Park. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the affected communities; it was issued at 1908MST and it ran through 1945MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the southern Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours, and they generated strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 45 mph. The gusty winds created dust storm conditions over portions of Yuma county, including the area around Tacna and Interstate 8. Although trained spotters in Tacna reported visibilities down to 1/4 mile in dense blowing dust, a Dust Storm Warning was not in effect. However, a Blowing Dust Advisory was issued for Yuma county at the time of the spotter report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon on September 6th, and due to the very hot and rather dry nature of the atmosphere, they generated very strong and gusty outflow winds. The gusty winds picked up copious amounts of dust and dirt and generated dust storm conditions which spread into the southwestern Arizona deserts during the evening hours. At 1913MST a trained weather spotter 2 miles southwest of Tacna reported dust storm conditions; visibility dropped to 1/4 mile and winds gusted to around 35 mph. A Dust Storm Warning had not been issued at the time, however there was a Blowing Dust Advisory in effect. The advisory was issued at 1817MST and continued until 2000MST. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the south central Arizona desert during the afternoon and evening hours on August 27th. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and as a result the thunderstorms were able to generate a wide variety of weather, including strong gusty winds, large hail and locally heavy rain. Most of the thunderstorms affected the area between Gila Bend and Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong gusty winds developed across portions of the south central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon hours on August 27th. Although no damage was reported due to the strong winds, there were multiple reports of gusts reaching at least 60 mph in strength. At 1630MST, a local post office in Stanfield estimated a peak gust to 60 mph. The wind created locally dense blowing dust which lowered visibility below one mile. At 1737MST, a trained weather spotter 15 miles southwest of the town of Maricopa estimated wind gusts of 60 mph at the intersection of North Warren and West Miller roads. The spotter also reported pea sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of southern Gila County during the evening hours on July 2nd. The storms affected the areas surrounding Globe, and they produced gusty and damaging wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across portions of southern Gila County and they affected the areas around Globe. The storms generated gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph. At 715 pm, a trained weather spotter east of Globe and about 1 mile west of Peridot reported that gusty winds downed an unknown number of trees and a number of branches with diameters between 4 and 6 inches. In addition, the winds damaged some shingles on a home and blew over an unknown type of a Trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the south central Arizona desert during the afternoon and evening hours on August 27th. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and as a result the thunderstorms were able to generate a wide variety of weather, including strong gusty winds, large hail and locally heavy rain. Most of the thunderstorms affected the area between Gila Bend and Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, some with large hail, developed across portions of northwestern Pinal county during the afternoon hours on August 27th. A trained weather spotter located 8 miles to the southwest of Ak-Chin Village reported dime to quarter sized hailstones along with locally heavy rainfall. He measured some of the larger hailstones at 1 inch diameter and also measured 0.54 inches of rain in a 15 minute period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across portions of the south central Arizona deserts on August 28th, and they affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The atmosphere was both very moist and unstable and this allowed thunderstorms to generate strong and damaging outflow winds in addition to heavy rain and flash flooding. The winds, estimated to be in excess of 60 mph, were strong enough to blow down dozens of large trees in the Waddell area around 8 pm. A variety of products were issued during the evening hours including Small Stream Flood Advisories, Flash Flood Warnings and Severe Thunderstorm Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the western portions of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on August 28th. Due to the very moist nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced locally heavy rains with rainfall rates reaching 2 inches per hour. At about 1930MST a trained spotter 4 miles west of Luke Air Force Base measured 2 inches of rain within one hour. Locally heavy rain continued in communities such as Litchfield Park, Goodyear and Buckeye. Due to the heavy rains, a Flash Flood Warning was issued at 2020MST for the west valley communities and it was in effect until 2215MST. It was later re-issued at 2248MST due to continued rainfall and remained in effect through 0145MST on August 29th. According to the department of highways, flash flooding occurred at 10 pm 4 miles west of Litchfield Park; the Loop 303 was closed between Interstate 10 and Glendale due to high flowing water. The closure lasted until approximately midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across portions of the south central Arizona deserts on August 28th, and they affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The atmosphere was both very moist and unstable and this allowed thunderstorms to generate strong and damaging outflow winds in addition to heavy rain and flash flooding. The winds, estimated to be in excess of 60 mph, were strong enough to blow down dozens of large trees in the Waddell area around 8 pm. A variety of products were issued during the evening hours including Small Stream Flood Advisories, Flash Flood Warnings and Severe Thunderstorm Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong ang gusty winds developed across the western portions of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on August 28th. The AWOS wind sensor at the Buckeye airport measured a wind gust to 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across the greater Phoenix area on August 26th, and some of them affected the community of Chandler. A trained weather spotter located about 4 miles southeast of Tumbleweed Park, or near the Chandler airport, measured a thunderstorm wind gust to 62 mph. A second trained weather spotter located 1 mile east of Chandler estimated a peak wind gust to 65 mph and the wind was strong enough to blow a 6 inch branch off of a tree. At the time, a Dust Storm Warning was  in effect. Also, a Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the Chandler area at 1821MST and it continued until 1845MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture from dissipating former tropical storm Ivo continued to stream into Arizona on August 24th, and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain developed during the day, leading to flash flooding. A large convective complex brought rains in excess of 2 inches to northwest Maricopa county in the morning, which resulted in flash flooding in the Wickenburg area. The focus for convection turned to La Paz county during the afternoon; scattered storms formed and led to additional heavy rains and flash flooding which affected communties along the Interstate 10 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of tropical moisture continued to spread into southern Arizona during the morning hours on August 24th, and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Scattered thunderstorms with locally heavy rain developed and moved into La Paz county after about 0630MST, and led to flooding of area roads and washes. Some of the areas hit the hardest were the Highway 60 and Interstate 10 corridors to the east of Brenda. A Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for central La Paz county beginning at 0628MST and it continued through 0930MST. According to a trained spotter located about 2 miles northwest of Wenden, 1.2 inches of rain fell in a 30 minute period ending at 0715MST and it was still raining hard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the morning hours on July 26th, and they continued into the afternoon. The storms affected southern Gila county, including areas from Globe to San Carlos, and also affected eastern portions of the greater Phoenix metropitan area including Apache Junction. The storms produced gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph as well as locally heavy rainfall with rain rates at least one inch per hour. The storms led to the issuance of a variety of products including Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms moved across portions of southern Gila county during the late morning hours on July 26th, and they affected areas from Globe southeastward to San Carlos Lake. The storms generated strong gusty winds, sufficient to down large trees. According to a trained weather spotter near San Carlos Lake or about 2 miles west of Peridot, at 1135 am gusty outflow winds downed a three foot diameter tree. The winds were estimated to be at least 60 mph. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect at the time, it was issued at 1106 am and continued through noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow spread copious amounts of tropical moisture northward and into the south central Arizona deserts during the morning hours on September 9th. Widespread showers with moderate to heavy rain developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and this led to episodes of flash flooding and urban flooding which started in the late morning hours. In addition to the showers, there were embedded thunderstorms which served to locally enhance rainfall totals. Local storm spotters along with Flood Control District gage reports indicated that between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell during the day. Numerous reports of flash flooding and urban flooding were received from across the greater Phoenix area, starting in the late morning and continuing into the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow imported copious amounts of tropical moisture into south central Arizona during the morning hours on September 9th, and as a result widespread showers developed across the central portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Areas of moderate to heavy rain developed, isolated embedded thunderstorms further enhanced the rainfall totals. Flood Control District gages and radar data indicated that rainfall rates up to 2 inches per hour had occurred during the late morning and this led to flash flooding between North Mountain Village and Deer Valley Village. According to the Arizona Department of Highways, at about 1200MST there was flooding near the intersection of Interstate 17 and Greenway Roads, about 3 miles north of Metro Center. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1133MST and it continued until 1300MST. Although the heavy rains did taper off by early afternoon, additional rain occurred into the afternoon hours, necessitating the issuance of another Urban and Small Stream Advisory at 1300MST. This new advisory continued through 1500MST. As an example of the amount of rain that fell with this flash flooding event, a trained weather spotter in the area measured a storm total of 2.42 inches of rain ending at 1615MST. His location was approximately 4 miles north of North Mountain Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture from dissipating former tropical storm Ivo continued to stream into Arizona on August 24th, and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain developed during the day, leading to flash flooding. A large convective complex brought rains in excess of 2 inches to northwest Maricopa county in the morning, which resulted in flash flooding in the Wickenburg area. The focus for convection turned to La Paz county during the afternoon; scattered storms formed and led to additional heavy rains and flash flooding which affected communties along the Interstate 10 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 24th, copious amounts of tropical moisture continued to spread into Arizona ahead of dissipating former tropical storm Ivo. This led to a very moist and unstable airmass, and scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours across portions of northwest Maricopa county, affecting the area around Wickenburg. Heavy rain began to fall from Aguila to Wickenburg beginning around 0400MST, and it continued to fall into the middle of the morning as a large convective complex developed. A flood advisory was issued for the area at 0524MST, and it was upgraded to a Flash Flood Warning at 0553MST. The warning remained in effect until shortly after 1000MST. According to the Wickenburg Fire Department and Rescue units, at 0730MST the Vulture Mine Road was closed around the Sols Wash due to high water. This was about 1 mile to the northwest of Wickenburg. At the same time, they reported that Rincon Road near Martinez Wash was also closed due to high water; this was about 2 miles north of Wickenburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of northern Pinal county during the afternoon hours on August 26th, and they affected the area around Casa Grande. Lingering tropical moisture made the airmass very moist and unstable, and the thunderstorms quickly generated heavy rain which was sufficient to produce flash flooding. At 1820MST, a trained weather spotter just north of Casa Grande reported water rapidly flowing down his street and additional water was flooding into numerous backyards. At 1815MST, another trained spotter near Sacaton reported water flowing rapidly across streets west of Sacaton, which made area roads impassable for at least 30 minutes. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1810MST and it continued through 2015MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture from dissipating former tropical storm Ivo continued to stream into Arizona on August 24th, and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Thunderstorms with very heavy rain developed during the day, leading to flash flooding. A large convective complex brought rains in excess of 2 inches to northwest Maricopa county in the morning, which resulted in flash flooding in the Wickenburg area. The focus for convection turned to La Paz county during the afternoon; scattered storms formed and led to additional heavy rains and flash flooding which affected communties along the Interstate 10 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture continued to flow into southern Arizona on August 24th, and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Scattered thunderstorms developed across much of La Paz county during the middle of the morning and they produced very heavy rain with rain rates in excess of 2 inches at times. The heavy rain affected both Highway 60 and Interstate 10 east of Brenda, and led to flash flooding which continued into the early afternoon hours. Small stream flood advisories were issued for portions of northeast and central La Paz counties starting at 0630MST, and they were upgraded to Flash Flood Warnings at 0933MST. Flash Flood Warnings for central La Paz county continued until about 1345MST. According to reports from the public, at 1100MST water was flowing across Avenue 42E and numerous washes east of Brenda were running heavily.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of southwest Maricopa county during the evening hours, and they affected the area around Gila Bend. Due in part to lingering tropical moisture, the storms were able to generate locally heavy rains which led to flash flooding. At 1845MST the Department of Highways reported that Highway 238 east of Gila Bend was closed due to flash flooding. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1836MST and it continued until 2030MST. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture ahead of dissipating former tropical storm Ivo streamed into southwestern Arizona on the 24th, and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Thunderstorms with heavy rain occurred during the evening hours, and the rain led to localized flooding and flash flooding which affected communities such as Somerton, Yuma and the Fortuna Foothills. Heavy rains led to washes running heavily, and water flowed across roads in the area. Fortunately, no accidents or injuries were reported as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of moisture spread north and into southwest Arizona on August 24th, ahead of dissipating former tropical storm Ivo. This led to a very moist and unstable airmass and scattered thunderstorms developed in the Yuma area during the evening hours. Locally heavy rain occurred, and radar indicated rain rates in excess of one inch per hour at times. The rain led to flooding and flash flooding of streets and washes in communities such as Yuma and the Fortuna Foothills. According to a trained weather spotter 2 miles east of the Fortuna Foothills, there was evidence of major flash flooding across county road 15E about 2 miles south of Interstate 8 during the evening hours. The report from the spotter was actually received around 1100MST on August 25th. Although a Flash Flood Warning was not in effect for the Yuma area during this event, an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory had been  issued at 1900MST and it continued until 2130MST. No reports of accidents or injuries were received due to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds kicked up a dust storm, reducing visibility to near zero on Pierce Ferry Road between Dolan Springs and Lake Mead City. The visibility contributed to a four-car crash which seriously injured two people.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near the intersection of Shaeffer Ave. and Norrie Dr.
