EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large push of monsoon moisture triggered an extended period of thunderstorms across the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than eight inches of water, mud and rocks flowed over DW Ranch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of far southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours on August 22nd, and they affected the community of Yuma, as well as surrounding communities such as Fortuna Foothills and Somerton. The thunderstorms were quite intense and produced a wide variety of weather, including strong damaging winds in excess of 50 mph, locally dense blowing dust, moderate hail and locally heavy rain. Winds were sufficient to blow down power lines and blow over trailer trucks. Hail nearly one inch in diameter fell in Yuma, and rainfall in the Fortuna Foothills approached 2 inches, leading to localized flooding. A wide variety of products were issued including Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across far southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours on August 22nd and they affected the community of Yuma. The storms generated strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which were sufficient to produce dust storm conditions with visibilities at or below one quarter of a mile in dense blowing dust. At 1652MST, a report was received via amateur radio, indicating a dust storm in Yuma with visibility down to around one quarter of a mile. A Dust Storm Warning was then issued at 1700MST for the Yuma area; it was in effect until 1835MST. The dust storm and associated low visibilities were a significant disruption to rush hour traffic in Yuma and impacted motorists on area roads and highways such as Interstate 8. Fortunately there were no reports of injuries or significant accidents due to the dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours to the south of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, and they produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph. The winds spread to the north and into the greater Phoenix area by about 1800MST, and as they moved in they generated dust storm conditions. At 1803MST a trained weather spotter 4 miles southeast of Tumbleweed Park in Chandler reported visibility down below 100 feet in dense blowing dust. Shortly thereafter he measured a wind gust to 62 mph. Another trained spotter 1 mile southeast of Ahwatukee reported a dust storm with visibility down to one quarter mile. Several other spotters in the Phoenix area reported dust storm conditions, and the winds were strong enough to uproot trees in Goodyear. A Dust Storm Warning was issued for the greater Phoenix area at 1741MST and it was cancelled at 1941MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large push of monsoon moisture triggered an extended period of thunderstorms across the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.

Southerly flow spread copious amounts of tropical moisture northward and into the south central Arizona deserts during the morning hours on September 9th. Widespread showers with moderate to heavy rain developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and this led to episodes of flash flooding and urban flooding which started in the late morning hours. In addition to the showers, there were embedded thunderstorms which served to locally enhance rainfall totals. Local storm spotters along with Flood Control District gage reports indicated that between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell during the day. Numerous reports of flash flooding and urban flooding were received from across the greater Phoenix area, starting in the late morning and continuing into the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow imported copious amounts of tropical moisture into south central Arizona during the morning hours on September 9th, and as a result widespread showers developed across the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Areas of moderate to heavy rain developed; isolated embedded thunderstorms further enhanced the rainfall totals. Flood Control District gages and radar data indicated that rainfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour had occurred during the late morning and this led to flash flooding in Apache Junction and Gold Canyon. According to a broadcast media report, at about 1215MST washes were running heavily in the Superstition Foothills area and water was flowing across roads and driveways. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued at 1109MST; it expired at 1230MST. Rainfall then tapered off but remained sufficiently heavy to warrant the issuance of another Urban and Small Stream Advisory at 1300MST. The advisory was converted to an Areal Flood Warning at 1430MST which ran until 1730MST. Fortunately no injuries were reported as a result of the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain along Highway 260 east of Camp Verde.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain (2.50 inches) caused water to flow across roads and several were barricaded.  A few homes in Camp Verde estates were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the south central Arizona desert during the afternoon and evening hours on August 27th. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and as a result the thunderstorms were able to generate a wide variety of weather, including strong gusty winds, large hail and locally heavy rain. Most of the thunderstorms affected the area between Gila Bend and Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across portions of northwest Pinal county on August 27th, and they produced gusty and damaging winds which affected Ak-Chin Village. A trained weather spotter 8 miles southwest of Ak-Chin Village reported that strong wind gusts and heavy rain blew down part of a porch roof. The winds were estimated to be in excess of 60 mph. Hail as large as 1 inch in diameter accompanied the strong winds. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the area starting at 1723MST and continuing until 1800MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the south central Arizona desert during the afternoon and evening hours on August 27th. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and as a result the thunderstorms were able to generate a wide variety of weather, including strong gusty winds, large hail and locally heavy rain. Most of the thunderstorms affected the area between Gila Bend and Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on August 27th, and they produced locally heavy rainfall which impacted the area between Gila Bend and the town of Maricopa. Due to the heavy rain, A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1842MST. At 1915MST, the Arizona Department of Highways reported that Highway 238 was closed between Gila Bend and Maricopa due to water flowing across the highway. The location of the report was about 3 miles southwest of the town of Mobile. The Flash Flood Warning was later cancelled at 1930MST as the rainfall had rapidly diminished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area of extreme drought (D3) conditions in southeast Arizona remained unchanged throughout August, essentially encompassing the southern halves of Graham and Greenlee counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area under extreme drought (D3) conditions remained unchanged as most locations in southern Graham and Greenlee counties received between 0.5 and 1.5 inches of rainfall during August, or about half of normal. The Safford airport received just 1.3 inches of rain. The Pinale��o Mountains were the exception to the dry trend where rainfall was near or even above normal. Some of the effects on the cotton crop have been mitigated by farmers leaving half of their fields idle this season. Mandatory water restrictions adopted by the City of Safford in August of 2012 have helped mitigate the city's supply to some extent, but due to the prolonged drought, the low water table caused the city to shut off four of the city's eleven wells. This in turn prompted the city to approve higher water rates to fund the drilling of new wells and building of pipelines. The estimated cost of a mile of new piping from proposed new wells south of the city was over $1 million. In nearby Thatcher, mandatory water restrictions were ordered and stricter water conservation standards have been adopted for new construction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure that had been centered over Arizona began to move northwest on July 1st. This caused moisture to move over the state from the east. Strong thunderstorms developed as a result of this moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported in University Heights south to Kachina Village. The Turkey Butte fire lookout tower suffered broken windows due to the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across portions of the south central Arizona deserts on August 28th, and they affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The atmosphere was both very moist and unstable and this allowed thunderstorms to generate strong and damaging outflow winds in addition to heavy rain and flash flooding. The winds, estimated to be in excess of 60 mph, were strong enough to blow down dozens of large trees in the Waddell area around 8 pm. A variety of products were issued during the evening hours including Small Stream Flood Advisories, Flash Flood Warnings and Severe Thunderstorm Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 28th, thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across the western portions of the greater Phoenix area, affecting communities such as Waddell, El Mirage and Luke Air Force Base. Due to the very unstable nature of the atmosphere, the stronger storms produced damaging wind gusts estimated to be at least 75 mph. At 1945MST, a trained weather spotter about 2 miles northwest of Waddell reported that dozens of trees were blown down with diameters as high as 3 feet. Shortly thereafter, a trained spotter 2 miles southwest of Luke AFB reported power poles blown down near Loop 303 and Bethany Home Road. Another trained spotter 2 miles northwest of Waddell reported that 7 to 8 eucalyptus trees were blown down. These trees were approximately 60 feet tall with diameters of 2 feet. Finally, at 2037MST a report was received via amatuer radio regarding large and mature Mesquite trees that were blown down 2 miles northwest of El Mirage. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the Luke and Waddell area, starting at 1923MST and continuing in effect through 2015MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on August 30th. The atmosphere was extremely moist and rather unstable and this allowed thunderstorms with strong gusty winds and heavy rain to develop. The storms primarily affected east and southeast valley communities such as Tempe, Chandler and Apache Junction. The stronger storms produced wet microbursts which downed a large number of trees in south Tempe around 5 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during Friday afternoon on August 30th, and they affected communities such as Tempe and Chandler. Due to the very moist and unstable character of the atmosphere, the storms were able to generate damaging wet microbursts, with wind gusts estimated to reach to near 70 mph. At 1710MST, a trained spotter reported trees downed in the vicinity of Priest and Ray Road. Heavy rain was also occurring leading to curb to curb flooding. The following day, a National Weather Service employee reported a number of large trees downed along Interstate 10 near Warner Road, in addition to large trees downed near the intersection of Ray and North Beck. Finally, the employee reported a tree 6 inches in diameter blown over on his property, at Priest Drive and West Maria Lane. The downed trees were a result of the wet microburst during the afternoon on August 30th.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon rainfall helped shrink the area of extreme drought (D3) conditions in southeast Arizona toward the end of July. Portions of Graham and Greenlee counties remained dry as the bulk of the rainfall occurred in Cochise County, especially near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area under extreme drought (D3) conditions shrank only slightly in southern Graham and Greenlee counties as the bulk of monsoon rain fell both to the north and south of the Gila River Valley. Most locations received between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rain during July, which is near or slightly below normal. The Safford airport received just 1.49 inches of rain.  Some of the effects on the cotton crop have been mitigated by farmers leaving half of their fields idle this season.  Mandatory water restrictions adopted by the City of Safford in August of 2012 have helped mitigate the city's supply to some extent, but due to the prolonged drought, the low water table caused the city to shut off four of the city's eleven wells. This in turn prompted the city to approve higher water rates to fund the drilling of new wells and building of pipelines. In nearby Thatcher, mandatory water restrictions were ordered and stricter water conservation standards have been adopted for new construction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure that had been centered over Arizona began to move northwest on July 1st. This caused moisture to move over the state from the east. Strong thunderstorms developed as a result of this moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a thunderstorm snapped a large (3.5 food diameter) cottonwood tree in half in Cornville.  A fence was also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts during the afternoon hours on September 6th and they affected portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The atmosphere was dry in the low levels, unstable, and very hot. Afternoon high temperatures in the central deserts reached or exceeded 110 degrees in the hottest locales. The combination of those conditions allowed the thunderstorms to generate strong and damaging winds, with peak gusts in excess of 50 mph. The strong winds were sufficient to knock down trees in central Phoenix. In addition, the gusty winds picked up significant amounts of dust and created dust storm conditions. Blowing Dust Advisories and Dust Storm Warnings were both issued for the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated afternoon thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts on September 6th, and they affected portions of the greater Phoenix area including Scottsdale. Due to the very hot and somewhat dry conditions, the thunderstorms generated gusty outflow winds of at least 45 mph. The strong wind gusts picked up copious amounts of dirt and dust and created dust storm conditions. At 1631MST a trained weather spotter 4 miles northeast of downtown Scottsdale reported visibility down to 1/4 mile at the intersection of Indian Bend Road the the Loop 101. The dust storm led to very hazardous driving conditions during the rush hour across the greater Phoenix area. At 1600MST, a Blowing Dust Advisory was issued for the south central deserts and it was upgraded to a Dust Storm Warning at 1630MST. The warning was in effect until 1900MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moccasin Wash Road was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level dry air pushed in above low level monsoon moisture, triggering widespread thunderstorms with flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to 10 inches of water and debris flowed over DW Ranch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona remained very moist and unstable, and as a result scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 20th. The storms generated locally heavy rain with rain rates in excess of 1 inch per hour, sufficient to cause local flooding of streams, washes and low lying areas. Heavy rain affected southern Gila county, especially the area around Miami. The heavy rain led to the issuance of multiple small stream flood advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southern Gila county during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 20th, and they generated locally heavy rain with rainfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour. Some of the heavier rain fell in the area around Globe and Miami. According to a trained weather spotter about 4 miles southwest of Miami, 1.61 inches of rain was measured between 530 pm and 6 pm. The heavy rain led to some flooding of area roads and washes; at 605 pm another trained spotter reported that the Miami wash in Miami was running bank to bank. The heavy rain and flooding led to the issuance of a Small Stream Flood Advisory for southern Gila county; it was issued at 545 pm and ran through 745 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level high pressure system developed across the desert southwest during the last few days of June 2013. This led to a protracted period of Excessive Heat over the lower Arizona deserts, which began on June 28, and continued into the first week of July. Many high temperature records were broken during the last weekend of June, and high temperatures reached or exceeded 120 degrees across some of the hottest deserts. Phoenix Sky Harbor reached to 119 degrees on June 29; this was a record high and the first time that a high of exactly 119 was recorded at Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level high pressure system developed across the desert southwest during the last few days of June, and this led to excessive heat across the lower south central Arizona deserts, beginning on June 28 and continuing through the end of the month. High temperatures at Phoenix Sky Harbor were at or above 115 degrees for the last 3 days of the month, including a record high of 119 on the 29th of June! That was the first time that a high of 119 was recorded at Phoenix. Low temperatures during the last weekend of the month exceeded 90 degrees in central Phoenix, offering little or no relief from the oppressive daytime heat. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued at 10 am Friday, June 28th and it ran through the end of June and into the first week of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure under westerly upper flow continued across the desert southwest. This caused high temperatures to be a few degrees above normal or in the 105-109 range June 16-18th. These hot temperatures possibly led to or played a role in the deaths of 4 believed to be immigrants in the desert near Gila Bend, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure through the middle part of June led to above normal temperatures, but a few degrees shy of excessive heat criteria. High temperatures from June 16-18th were in the 105-109 range across the southern Arizona Deserts. Maricopa County Sheriff's deputies reported finding 4 male bodies on June 20th in the desert near mile post 133 along Interstate 8 outside of Gila Bend. They indicated the men had died at least three days before the bodies were found, so likely in the June 16-17 time frame. Officials believed the men were immigrants who had recently crossed the Mexico/U.S. border. Cause of death is currently not known, but heat was presumed to be at least partially to blame as there were no signs of foul play.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms, with locally heavy rain, developed over the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 15th. Some of the storms produced gusty and damaging winds, estimated to reach or exceed 60 mph. The intensity of the storms warranted the issuance of several products such as Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban Flood Advisories. The gusty winds blew down a large number of trees in the greater Phoenix area, and even blew a semi trailer-truck off of Interstate 10 near Wild Horse Pass road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, and they affected communities such as Ahwatukee and Firebird Lake. The storms produced gusty winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph. According to local broadcast media reports, at 505 pm a semi truck was blown off of Interstate 10 and overturned near 1-10 and Wild Horse Pass Road, approximately 1 mile north of Firebird Lake. No injuries were reported. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning had been issued for the area beginning at 454 pm and continuing until 545 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reached Excessive Heat Warning criteria for four consecutive days. Two people died from the heat and eight others were injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reached Excessive Heat Warning criteria for four consecutive days.  One man died from the heat and five others were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure brought record-breaking temperatures to much of the Desert Southwest.  Several people died from the heat, many more were injured, and some infrastructure was damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached June 28th through 30th, and continued into July. The heat knocked out phone and internet service to Lake Havasu City for several hours on the morning of the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure brought record-breaking temperatures to much of the Desert Southwest.  Several people died from the heat, many more were injured, and some infrastructure was damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached each day from June 28th through 30th, and continued into July. Three people were overcome by heat in Bullhead City on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours across southern Gila county on July 10th. The storms generated strong and gusty winds, and also moderate to large hail. No damage was reported due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours across southern Gila County on July 10th. Some of the storms affected the areas around Hayden and Winkelman. At 603 pm, a public report was received concerning strong gusty winds 1 mile southeast of Hayden; a thunderstorm wind gust was estimated at approximately 60 mph. No damage was reported due to the strong gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy snow fell as a strong upper level low pressure system moved through the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated six inches of snow fell at Hualapai Mountain Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and very cold area of low pressure developed across the desert southwest during the middle of January 2013, which led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across the lower deserts. Freezing temperatures developed on the morning of January 12, and continued for 4 days. The coldest morning was on January 15, the final day of the freeze outbreak, and the temperature that morning at Phoenix Sky Harbor fell to 29 degrees. The freezing temperatures resulted in significant damage across the deserts, in the form of burst outdoor pipe, crop damage and dead frost sensitive plants and trees, such a ficus and lantana. Freeze, and hard freeze, warnings were issued for the deserts of Arizona during this cold temperature outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Wednesday January 16th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across much of southwestern Maricopa county, including the community of Gila Bend. A Freeze Warning was issued for the area beginning Friday night and running through Wednesday morning on January 16th. On Tuesday January 15th, the warning was upgraded to a Hard Freeze Warning, but the warning returned to a regular Freeze Warning for Wednesday. The colder desert area of southwest Maricopa county fell into the upper teens to mid 20s each morning during the freeze event; on Tuesday morning the Gila Bend airport fell to 19 degrees which represented the coldest of the 5 mornings. The extreme cold caused pipes to burst and led to losses of landscaping plants and trees, garden plants and vegetables, and citrus crops.

The freezing temperatures resulted in significant damage across the deserts, in the form of burst outdoor pipe, crop damage and dead frost sensitive plants and trees, such a ficus and lantana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Monday January 14th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across much of the lower Colorado River Valley, including the communities of Parker and Cibola. A Freeze Warning was issued for the area beginning Friday night and running through Monday morning on January 14th. Increased wind led to the cancellation of the Freeze Warning during the early afternoon on Monday. The colder areas along the lower Colorado river valley fell into the 20s each morning; the extreme cold caused some pipes to burst and led to losses of landscaping plants and trees, garden plants and vegetables, and citrus crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold low temperatures persisted across much of southeast Arizona for several nights.  One fatality was attributed to the very cold temperatures.  Most of the damage consisted of broken water pipes. However the extent of damage was less than in February of 2011 because daytime temperatures recovered into the 40s and 50s during most days.  Also, many of the pipes that were damaged during the 2011 freeze were fortified against cold temperatures and were not damaged again this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in the teens or lower 20s for several nights caused numerous pipes to burst in the Tucson metropolitan area.  The Tucson International Airport dropped to 15 degrees on the morning of January 15th. Most of the frozen pipes exposed to the cold were on the roofs or sides of homes.  Also, citrus fruits were damaged by the hard freeze, which meant that local food banks could not glean unpicked fruit to supplement their food donations. Total damage was estimated at $100,000.  Additionally, two house fires were indirectly related to the cold weather.  A mobile home caught fire when the owner attempted to thaw frozen pipes with a propane torch. Another home caught fire after residents placed a heat lamp and blankets on a patio overnight to keep pets warm. No one was injured in either fire.

A death due to exposure occurred the morning of January 16th east of Dudleyville, in the southwest corner of the San Carlos Apache Reservation. The deceased was not a member of the tribe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm moist southerly flow over northern Arizona brought areas of persistent moderate to heavy rain.  The low pressure center responsible for this southwest flow moved across the state on January 27 with precipitation tapering off in the afternoon. A cold front then moved across the state with more rain for the lower elevations and lowering slow levels overnight January 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain showers developed over Apache County during the early hours of January 26 and continued through the 28th. The heaviest rain fell over orographically favored areas.  The greatest rainfall totals include: 2 miles north of McNary 1.65 inches, Tsaile 1.15 inches, Canyon de Chelly 1.11 inches, and Window Rock Airport 1.09. The snow level remained high for most of this event. Snow reports include: Sunrise Ski 6.0 inches and Window Rock 3 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate rain fell across Coconino County for several days with the snow level above 8500 feet for much of the time. Some rainfall amounts (in inches) include: Flagstaff Airport 2.56, Fort Valley 2.44, Kachina Village 2.23, Bellemont 2.48, Limestone Canyon 1.78, Valle 1.59, Blue Ridge 1.49, Page 0.72, and Lees Ferry 0.59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved across Southeast Arizona on February 20th resulting in very low snow levels across the region. Snow levels dropped as low as 2000 feet with measurable snow at elevations 2500 feet and higher including the Tucson metro area. Many schools in southeast Arizona either closed February 20th and/or had delayed starts February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snowfall occurred across the lower elevations of Santa Cruz county and the Altar Valley in Southern Pima County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved east across the central Arizona deserts on the 8th of March, and the associated cold front passed over the area during the afternoon hours bringing gusty winds in excess of 50 mph, locally heavy rain and small to moderate hail. The cold front generated scattered thunderstorms, which produced locally damaging winds in the greater Phoenix area, and locally heavy thunderstorm rains caused some flooding of streets and washes. Flooding of washes occurred in the community of Scottsdale, and one woman was found dead in one of the flooded washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, associated with a passing cold front, moved across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on March 8th. Some of the storms affected southeast valley communities such as Chandler, and they produced locally strong and gusty outflow winds. According to a trained weather spotter about 1 mile northwest of Tumbleweed Park in Chandler, at 245  pm wind gusts estimated to be at least 60 mph caused a 10 inch diameter Palo Verde tree to be uprooted and knocked down. Another trained spotter 1 mile east of Chandler also reported a 6 inch diameter tree blown down at 245 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely cold trough of low pressure from the Pacific moved over the western United states for several days. A series of disturbances moved through this trough producing several rounds of heavy snow over northern Arizona. Some locations had a brief break in snow fall while other locations only saw periods of heavy snow between lighter periods of snow. This trough will be treated as one episode because of the difficulty in separating the events into different episodes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Happy Jack Ranger Station reported a storm total of 24 inches of new snow, Forest Lakes had 15 inches, and Clints Well reported 10 inches, Linden (2ESE) had 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved across Southeast Arizona on February 20th resulting in very low snow levels across the region. Snow levels dropped as low as 2000 feet with measurable snow at elevations 2500 feet and higher including the Tucson metro area. Many schools in southeast Arizona either closed February 20th and/or had delayed starts February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall occurred across the mountains of Cochise County including 6 inches in the Bisbee area. The Mule Pass Tunnel on Highway 80 was closed due to the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely cold trough of low pressure from the Pacific moved over the western United states for several days. A series of disturbances moved through this trough producing several rounds of heavy snow over northern Arizona. Some locations had a brief break in snow fall while other locations only saw periods of heavy snow between lighter periods of snow. This trough will be treated as one episode because of the difficulty in separating the events into different episodes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across northern Gila County with the lower elevations reaching heavy snow criteria sooner than higher elevations. Snowfall totals include (in inches): Whispering Pines 20, Tonto Village 16, Pine (5800 feet) 10,  Payson (14 NNE) 12, Tonto Natural Bridge 9.2, and Payson (4900 feet) 7.9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely cold trough of low pressure from the Pacific moved over the western United states for several days. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mountainaire and Williams reported 7.5 and 6.8 inches of new snow by 600 PM February 9 with the first round of snow in a series of disturbances moving across northern Arizona. Both locations are between 5000 feet and 7000 feet.  By the morning of February 11 the storm moved east and left a blanket of snow across the area. Total snowfall amounts include:  Snowbowl Ski Resort 19 inches at 10800 feet and 10 inches at 9000 feet, 18.2 inches 5 miles south of Williams, 11 inches in Williams, 14 inches in Mountainaire, 13.3 inches at the Flagstaff Airport, 10.4 inches in Kachina Village, and 9.2 inches in Bellemont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and cold upper level low pressure center move southeast along the West Coast then eastward across Arizona. This brought areas of heavy snow to much of the area. Several weaker disturbances followed the main low with a continuation of snow showers with locally heavy accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong upslope flow enhanced snowfall over Northern Gila County along the Mogollon Rim. Snowfall amounts (in inches) include: Strawberry/Pine 8-10, Payson 6-9, Tonto Village 8.5, and Whispering Pines 8-9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system moving through the desert southwest on April 8th led to the development of strong, gusty and damaging southwest to west winds across portions of south central Arizona, including southern Gila county and the greater Phoenix area. The strong wind led to the issuance of a variety of products during the afternoon hours, including Wind Advisories and Blowing Dust Advisories. Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were common and were sufficiently strong to cause isolated damage to trees and weaker structures in local communities such as Globe. Wind damage was not a result of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm moved slowly across the desert southwest on April 8th, bringing strong and gusty southwest to west winds to the central Arizona deserts. A wind advisory was issued for the greater Phoenix area starting at 10 am, and continuing through 8 pm. Peak wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were common during the day. At 130 pm, a trained spotter 4 miles southeast of Tumbleweed Park reported that strong winds, estimated to be near 50 mph, blew down a large tree near the intersection of Riggs and Gilbert Roads. The large tree knocked down a brick wall as it fell. No injuries were reported. Tumbleweed park is located to the south of Chandler, and to the northeast of Sun Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated across parts of southeast Arizona, including lower elevations, on February 11th. While the snow melted and roads generally dried out, frost formed on bridge decks and overpasses during the overnight hours, causing hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist lost control of their vehicle when they encountered black ice that had formed on Interstate 19 near the San Xavier exit. The result was a ten vehicle accident. One person suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to much of northern Arizona March 8 and 9. The heaviest snow fell above 6000 where most of the precipitation through the event was snow. Between 4000 feet and 6000 feet, the precipitation started out as rain and then changed to snow as the main part of the storm moved over Arizona. Accumulating snow was reported as low as 3800 feet before the storm moved east. Snowfall amounts ranged from around an inch in Sedona, Show Low, and Ganado to around two feet in Forest Lakes, Sunrise Ski Park, and Arizona Snowbowl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals (in inches) include: Arizona Snowbowl 21, Flagstaff 16, Flagstaff Airport 15.2, Williams 6, Kachina Village 12, Bellemont 12, and Munds Park 10.8. The Department of Public Safety reported at least 60 slide offs, 20 collisions, 14 motorist assists, and three injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North winds behind a cold front funneled down the Colorado River Valley, producing isolated high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 4 miles N of Mohave Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved across Southeast Arizona March 8th into March 9th resulting in significant snow in the higher elevations. In addition, there were numerous small hail reports with convective showers in the lower elevations on March 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Mountains of Greenlee County received significant snowfall with the Hannagan Meadow SNOTEL receiving 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably strong high pressure aloft developed across the desert southwest on Wednesday, June 6th, and it led to very hot temperatures across the central Arizona deserts including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. High temperatures in the hotter deserts reached to around 110 degrees and this led to the issuance of an Excessive Heat Warning for the afternoon and early evening hours. The extreme heat led to the death of a Spanish tourist who was attempting to hike Table Top mountain near Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat developed across the central Arizona deserts on Wednesday, June 12th and it led to the issuance of an Excessive Heat Warning which ran from 10 am until 8 pm. High temperatures reached to around 110 degrees over the hotter deserts; in fact the official high temperature at Phoenix was 110 for the day. According to an on-line article posted at MyFoxPhoenix.Com, a 50 year old professor visiting Arizona from Spain died of heat exhaustion on Wednesday afternoon while attempting to hike Table Top Mountain locate near Casa Grande. At about 430 pm, the Pinal County Sheriff's office received a call about a man who had passed out while hiking. The victim and 2 friends set out for a day hike; they begain the 3 and one half mile hike during the morning and when they reached the top, they stopped for lunch. On the way back down, the 50 year old collapsed. He was unable to walk and appeared disoriented. His friends attempted to carry him down the trail but he lost conciousness; one of the men ran for help and the other performed CPR. Search and Rescue and Emergency Medical Units arrived but the man was pronounced dead. Deputies said that the men did have water but it was not enough for the extreme summer heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions deteriorated into extreme drought conditions over portions of  southeast Arizona toward the end of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While much of southeast Arizona benefited from near or even slightly above normal precipitation in December of 2012 into January of 2013, valleys of Cochise, Graham and Greenlee Counties remained on the dry side.  February and April totals were less than 50% of normal in these areas, and they fared only marginally better during March.  By late April, extreme drought (D3) conditions developed over the northeast three quarters of Cochise County and the southern halves Graham and Greenlee Counties, essentially east of a line from Clifton to Fort Thomas to Benson to Bisbee. One impact was an increase in the wildfire danger in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little if any rainfall occurred during May. Thus, extreme drought conditions continued across far southeastern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Since little or no rain fell during May, extreme drought (D3) conditions remained in place across the northeast three quarters of Cochise County and the southern halves of Graham and Greenlee Counties. Wildfire danger remained a concern and a couple of human caused wildfires did develop. Fortunately, wind conditions were not extreme and rapid fire spread did not occur.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds were sustained at times between 35 and 45 mph with gusts around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning injured four people at Mather Point at the Grand Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1:00 p.m. on Monday, July 15 a lightning strike near Mather Point resulted in several visitors reporting injuries to the Grand Canyon Visitors Center. The group of visitors was standing near the Rim when the lightning strike occurred. Four individuals were transported to the South Rim Clinic for evaluation of non-life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from southern Nevada across northern Arizona bringing strong southwest winds to southeast Arizona. While the winds did not bring any major damage, they did generate areas of blowing dust that led to motor vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds around 30 mph with gusts to 50 mph led to areas of blowing dust across Interstate 10 in Pinal County. Visibility was restricted to less than 100 feet at times. Two semis jack-knifed and were involved in an accident with two other passenger vehicles near Picacho and Milepost 213. Four minor injuries resulted.  Another multi-vehicle chain-reaction accident on westbound Interstate 10 occurred near Milepost 212 resulted in less serious damage and no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures and light surface winds were conducive to dust devil development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly translucent, but powerful dust devil damaged the roof to one home on Tucson's east side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures and light surface winds were conducive to dust devil development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful dust devil removed several dozen roof tiles from two homes in Green Valley. Wind speeds were estimated at 65 mph.  A stove vent was also blown off one roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions and moderate winds resulted in this human-caused fire spreading among dry fuels in the San Pedro River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The human-caused Benson 1 Fire started near Interstate 10, just west of Pomerene, AZ along the dry San Pedro River bed and spread through timber and brush near the river bottom. Since it was difficult to access, moderate winds caused this fire to spread up the banks where it destroyed a small trailer at an RV park.  As a precautionary measure, a two mile stretch of Interstate 10 was closed briefly on the day the fire started, and about 25 mobile homes were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and dry air mass brought the coldest temperatures in six years across the Mojave Desert.  Hard freezes occurred on four to five consecutive mornings in most places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were at least six reports of water pipes bursting in Lake Havasu City due to cold temperatures.  Some of the pipes were in attics of homes, resulting in water damage to the homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and dry air mass brought the coldest temperatures in six years across the Mojave Desert.  Hard freezes occurred on four to five consecutive mornings in most places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were at least 50 reports of burst or frozen pipes in Kingman due to the cold.  The entire water system of one hotel froze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and very cold area of low pressure developed across the desert southwest during the middle of January 2013, which led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across the lower deserts. Freezing temperatures developed on the morning of January 12, and continued for 4 days. The coldest morning was on January 15, the final day of the freeze outbreak, and the temperature that morning at Phoenix Sky Harbor fell to 29 degrees. The freezing temperatures resulted in significant damage across the deserts, in the form of burst outdoor pipe, crop damage and dead frost sensitive plants and trees, such a ficus and lantana. Freeze, and hard freeze, warnings were issued for the deserts of Arizona during this cold temperature outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Wednesday January 16th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across the central deserts, including the communities of San Tan Valley, Casa Grande, Coolidge, Florence, Arizona City and Maricopa. A Hard Freeze warning was issued beginning Saturday night, which continued through the morning hours on Monday, January 14th. The Hard Freeze warning was downgraded to a regular Freeze Warning beginning Monday night, continuing through Wednesday morning January 16th. Despite the downgrade, the  coldest morning of the freezing period was Tuesday, January 15, when the low dropped to 16 degrees in the town of Maricopa, and down to a frigid 13 degrees at Casa Grande National Monument. Overall, low temperatures each morning fell into the upper teens to low 20s over the colder deserts, resulting in the destruction of garden plants and vegetables as well as damage to landscaping plants and citrus trees. In addition, many outdoor pipes were damaged or burst completely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm moist southerly flow over northern Arizona brought areas of persistent moderate to heavy rain.  The low pressure center responsible for this southwest flow moved across the state on January 27 with precipitation tapering off in the afternoon. A cold front then moved across the state with more rain for the lower elevations and lowering slow levels overnight January 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Northern Gila County.  Some of the rainfall amounts (in inches) include: Tonto Creek Fish Hatchery 4.97, Red Lake 3.22, Pleasant Valley 2.36, and Payson Airport 2.13.

Freeze, and hard freeze, warnings were issued for the deserts of Arizona during this cold temperature outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Monday January 14th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing temperatures across much of southwest Yuma county including the communities of  Yuma and Martinez Lake. A Freeze Warning was issued for the area beginning Friday night and running through early Monday morning on January 14th. Increasing wind and cloudiness brought a premature ending to the Freeze Warning, it was cancelled at 4 am on Monday morning. The colder areas of southwest Yuma county fell into the mid 20s to around 30 during the mornings of the freeze event; the Yuma Red Bluff Pavement COOP station reported a low of 24 degrees on Sunday morning. The cold temperatures caused some damage to exposed pipes as well as causing the loss of landscaping plants and trees, garden plants and vegetables, and citrus crops.

The coldest morning was on January 15, the final day of the freeze outbreak, and the temperature that morning at Phoenix Sky Harbor fell to 29 degrees. Freeze, and hard freeze, warnings were issued for the deserts of Arizona during this cold temperature outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Wednesday January 16th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The low temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor fell below freezing on 4 consecutive days, with the coldest morning low of 29 degrees recorded on January 15th. The colder outlying areas of the Phoenix area fell into the low to mid 20s each morning, and the extreme cold caused many pipes to burst, and led to citrus crop losses as well as the death of many frost sensitive plants and trees such as ficus and lantana. The freeze event led to the issuance of a continuous freeze warning for the Phoenix area beginning Friday night January 11th and running through Wednesday morning on Janaury 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Wednesday January 16th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across much of La Paz county, including the communities of Bouse and Quartzsite. A Hard Freeze warning was issued for the area beginning Saturday night and running through Monday morning on January 14th, a regular Freeze Warning was in effect for the remaining mornings during the freeze event. The colder desert areas of La Paz county fell into the middle teens to lower 20s each morning; on Sunday morning Bouse fell to 16 degrees which was the coldest morning of the freeze event. The extreme cold caused many pipes to burst and led to losses of landscaping plants and trees, garden plants and vegetables, and citrus crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and very cold area of low pressure developed across the desert southwest during the middle of January 2013, which led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across the lower deserts. Freezing temperatures developed on the morning of January 12, and continued for 4 days. The coldest morning was on January 15, the final day of the freeze outbreak, and the temperature that morning at Phoenix Sky Harbor fell to 29 degrees. The freezing temperatures resulted in significant damage across the deserts, in the form of burst outdoor pipe, crop damage and dead frost sensitive plants and trees, such a ficus and lantana. Freeze, and hard freeze, warnings were issued for the deserts of Arizona during this cold temperature outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass settled in across the desert southwest from Saturday January 12th through Wednesday January 16th. The cold and relatively dry conditions led to widespread freezing and sub-freezing temperatures across much of northwestern Maricopa county, including the communities of Wickenburg, Buckeye and New River.  A Hard Freeze Warning was issued for the area beginning Saturday night and running through Monday morning. It was downgraded to a regular Freeze Warning which continued through Wednesday morning on January 16th. The colder desert area of northwest Maricopa county fell into the upper teens to mid 20s each morning; on Tuesday morning Wickenburg airport fell to 17 degrees. The extreme cold caused many pipes to burst and led to losses of landscaping plants and trees, garden plants and vegetables, and citrus crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms, with locally heavy rain, developed over the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 15th. Some of the storms produced gusty and damaging winds, estimated to reach or exceed 60 mph. The intensity of the storms warranted the issuance of several products such as Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban Flood Advisories. The gusty winds blew down a large number of trees in the greater Phoenix area, and even blew a semi trailer-truck off of Interstate 10 near Wild Horse Pass road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the greater Phoenix area during the late afternoon hours on July 15th, and they affected the community of Ahwatukee. The storms produced gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 70 mph. According to broadcast media reports and supported by aerial video, 4 homes under construction in a subdivison directly west of Desert View High School between E. Frye Rd. and E. Glenhaven Rd. were destroyed and an additional 6 homes (further along in the construction process had minor damage. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning had been issued for the area starting at 454 pm and continuing until 545 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in west and northeast Flagstaff. One inch diameter hail was also reported in east Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over the hills north of West Route 66. The runoff caused damage to 5 properties between 130 PM and 200 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in west and northeast Flagstaff. One inch diameter hail was also reported in east Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail fell in east Flagstaff along with very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area of extreme drought (D3) conditions encompassing the southern halves of Graham and Greenlee counties was downgraded to the severe drought (D2) category by the middle of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area under extreme drought (D3) conditions in southern Graham and Greenlee Counties was downgraded to severe drought (D2) conditions my mid September. The D2 area also contained northern portions of Cochise and Pima as well as southern Pinal counties. Between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell in early September across the D3 area, with some locations near Safford receiving nearly twice the normal monthly rainfall amount during this time period. The Safford Airport received 2.66 inches of rainfall from September 6th through the 16th. In addition, very heavy rains in western New Mexico and along the Mogollon Rim filled the Gila River during September. Stretches of the Gila River actually rose to above bank full for several days.  While the shorter term drought issues were eased, long term drought remains a concern.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major flash flood hit Antelope Canyon with water hitting the underside of a bridge. The water in the normally dry slot canyon is over 40 feet in places. Flood waters in Honey Draw were running over State Route 99 and and State Highway 89.  The water and mud flowed down State Highway 89 and into a gas station at Lake Powell Boulevard. The mud that was deposited in the parking lot was 12-18 inches deep and trapped vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeasterly surface winds in a warm and moist environment helped storms build over higher terrain of southern Arizona. Storms moved west into valley locations and produced localized wind damage, especially in central and western Pima County where thunderstorms became more widespread.

Part of the barn roof struck a moving vehicle, denting the car and breaking its windshield. No injuries occurred. A tree was also blown over near Tanque Verde Loop Road south of Speedway Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain about 15 miles north-northeast of Flagstaff.  A Forest Service road was washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forest Road 514 was washed out and was impassible about 1/3 of a mile west of Highway 89.  Radar estimated that 1.75 inches of rain fell in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeasterly surface winds in a warm and moist environment helped storms build over higher terrain of southern Arizona. Storms moved west into valley locations and produced localized wind damage, especially in central and western Pima County where thunderstorms became more widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds blew the lightly-nailed wooden roof off a church at Ak-Chin Village. Two other structures had portions of their roofs blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper pattern was defined by a strong ridge of high pressure over the middle of the country, and a trough off the coast of the Pacific Northwest. An overnight MCS moved from southeast to northwest from Sonora, Mexico into southern Arizona. The leading edge of the complex caused wind damage in Nogales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew off a portion of a roof in Nogales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With high amplitude ridge still over the plains, warm temperatures and ample moisture again contributed to scattered thunderstorms. A few thunderstorms produced wind damage to trees and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed nine power poles near Pima Mine Road and Nogales Highway. The downed poles caused 11,000 customers to be without power for several hours and both roadways were closed through the night. A roof was blown off a building at the Sahuarita waste-water treatment plant.  In addition, the roof was ripped off a mobile home on East Lumber Street near Old Nogales Highway. Several other mobile homes sustained minor damage in that area and one tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With high amplitude ridge still over the plains, warm temperatures and ample moisture again contributed to scattered thunderstorms. A few thunderstorms produced wind damage to trees and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed or bent several power poles on Valencia Road west of Casino Del Sol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture from a strong push in late August continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert through early September. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Sacramento Wash basin produced high flows in the wash, which covered the Oatman-Topock Highway with mud and water and damaged 50 boat slips at the Topock 66 Spa and Resort on the Colorado River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture from a strong push in late August continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert through early September. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of DW Ranch Road was washed out after heavy rain fell on the Dean Peak burn area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: DW Ranch Road was washed out between mile markers 3 and 5 after heavy rain fell on the Dean Peak burn area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture from a strong push in late August continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert through early September. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was stuck in a flooded wash on Stockton Hill Road near mile marker 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture from a strong push in late August continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert through early September. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pierce Ferry Road was closed at mile marker 17 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days in a row of monsoon thunderstorms occurred in east central Arizona and west central New Mexico. Runoff drained into the Blue, San Francisco and Gila Rivers. The San Francisco River at Clifton approached flood stage, reaching the bottom of the railroad bridge, and the Highway 191 gates were closed as a precaution, but the only flood damage that occurred was upstream along the Blue River in Greenlee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the evening of September 13th, heavy rain occurred along the Campbell Blue Creek near the headwaters of the Blue River in Arizona, which was an area that was affected by the 2011 Wallow Fire. Heavy rain also fell along Turkey Creek in Arizona and the Dry Blue Creek in New Mexico.  Runoff continued downstream to the confluence of the Campbell Blue and Dry Blue Creeks, which forms the Blue River.  In addition, heavy rain occurred during the evening of the 14th in Arizona and New Mexico, swelling the tributaries of the Blue River upstream of the community of Blue, Arizona.  Multiple crests in the Blue River two to four feet above flood stage resulted. Sections of Blue River Road were washed out, isolating residents of the community.  The Campbell Blue Bridge, which is a 30 foot concrete bridge just across the state line in New Mexico, was completely washed out. Electrical and telephone lines were damaged by the flooding. A utility company truck rolled into Turkey Creek after the adjacent flood-compromised road gave way.  The driver was able to escape the six foot deep flowing water without injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding in Antelope Canyon and Cibecue Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 1 to 2 foot rise on Cibecue Creek was reported near Cibecue.  A five foot rise was reported on a stream gauge downstream of Cibecue. The drainage area was burned in 2002 in the Rodeo-Chediski Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and flash flooding between the Colorado River and the South Rim.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm created a large wall of water that carried large boulders and debris through Indian Gardens below the South Rim of the Grand Canyon. Trails were washed out. The water was estimated to be 8 to 10 feet deep in places. Campers were trapped in the Indian Gardens Campground during the flooding.  The peak of the flooding was at 300 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the Dilkon area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water and debris closed the main road in Dilkon for an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding about 10 miles northeast of Show Low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters flowed over the bridge over Silver Creek on Bourden Ranch Road northeast of Show Low.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorms produced heavy rain in and around Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: O'Leary Peak received 1.88 inches of rain in 45 minutes at the fire lookout. Pea sized hail covered the ground half an inch deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorms produced heavy rain in and around Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (2.25 inches) from a thunderstorm fell in Fort Valley.  Hail 1/4 inch in diameter also fell during the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure centered just north of the four corners region along with low pressure located over the southern Nevada helped pull monsoonal moisture northward from Mexico into southern Arizona, fueling storms with damaging thunderstorm winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree near Speedway Blvd. and Stone Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure developed over California and Arizona for several days. This brought very hot conditions to northern Arizona with many locations breaking daily high temperature records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff Airport reached a high of 91 which broke the record high of 89 for the day last set in 1985. The Flagstaff Airport is at an elevation of 7003 feet above sea level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure developed over California and Arizona for several days. This brought very hot conditions to northern Arizona with many locations breaking daily high temperature records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff Airport reached a high of 90 which broke the old record high of 88 for the day last set in 1902.  Fort Valley Experimental Station reached a high of 91 which broke the record high of 89 for the day last set in 1996.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorms produced heavy rain in and around Flagstaff.
