EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud flowed over multiple low water crossings on Stockton Hill Road, forcing it to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. A few storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 12 inches of water with rocks and mud flowed down streets in Lake Havasu City. Multiple roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain in and around Ash Fork. This caused flash flooding that impacted the town of Ash Fork, Interstate 40, Partridge Creek, and Big Chino Wash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain, up to four inches in two hours, fell in and around Ash Fork with flash flooding.  Interstate 40 was closed from mile marker 140 to 144 for two and a half hours because of all the water and debris on the roadway.  Five inches to a foot of standing water was reported in business on the west side of town. Double Ranch Road was under water. Flooding was reported in the Kaibab Subdivision.  The flooding in Ash Fork was slowly receding by 945 PM. The flood waters Ash Fork flowed down Ash Fork Draw, Partridge Creek and into Big Chino Wash all the way down to near Paulden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen just east of Flagstaff from Winona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The funnel cloud started as a large bowl shaped cloud, lowered, and then sharpened into a tight funnel and then dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the Grand Canyon National Park and on the Havasupai Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused Havasu Creek to rise 5 feet through the Village of Supai.  Minor damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the Grand Canyon National Park and on the Havasupai Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced flooding in Garden Creek along the popular Bright Angel Trail. The depth of the flood waters through Indian Gardens was 3 to 5 feet deep. The first flash flood on Garden Creek was reported at 1238 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Antelope Canyon just east of Page.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding out of Upper Antelope Canyon closed Highway 98 east of Page. Several thunderstorms formed over the Antelope Canyon drainage though the afternoon and continued the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across the Navajo Nation. Several communities were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding across the Navajo Nation. Road damage from flooding prevented travel in Red Valley. Water entered a store, roads were damaged, and bridge was damaged in Mexican Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across the Navajo Nation. Several communities were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen sheep were killed by lightning.  Time and location are estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Show Low Airport recorded a peak wind gust of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Limestone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding in the Prescott area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain fell in half an hour near Williamson Vally road just north of Prescott. There was four feet of water in the normally dry wash. Logs and cars were carried off of roads. A flash flood in Prescott carried a car down a flooded stream.  A swift water rescue successfully recovered the occupants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along Highway 191 from around Chinle northward to around Round Rock. The heavy rain also caused an earthen dam to break in this area. Heavy rain also impacted portions of northern Apache County near Highway 160.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A box culvert was overcome by flood water near mile post 417 on Highway 160. Several local residences and the Chapter House were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced severe hail and flash flooding near the town of Whiteriver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported 3 miles east of Whiteriver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along Highway 191 from around Chinle northward to around Round Rock. The heavy rain also caused an earthen dam to break in this area. Heavy rain also impacted portions of northern Apache County near Highway 160.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Businesses were flooded at the junction of Highway 191 and Hospital Drive.  Flooding was also reported on Highway 191 at mile post 446.8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along Highway 191 from around Chinle northward to around Round Rock. The heavy rain also caused an earthen dam to break in this area. Heavy rain also impacted portions of northern Apache County near Highway 160.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from an earthen dam caused flooding of several residences and mobile homes at 200 PM near US Highway 191 at mile post 467.2.  The dam broke around noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced scattered heavy rain across northern Arizona. Some storms lasted through the night. Some locations (Cottonwood, Sedona, Heber-Overgaard, and Parks) received an inch to an inch and a half in 24 hours without any flooding issues. Other locations had flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported flooding along Road Five North in Chino Valley. A woman wading downstream in Granite Creek was washed away by flood waters. Her body was found later that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced severe hail and flash flooding near the town of Whiteriver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding and debris was reported on several area roads. Three homes were flooded in East Fork and one in Sevenmile. The amount of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong upper level low pressure system developed across the desert southwest on October 9th and it led to strong and gusty winds across the central Arizona deserts during the afternoon and evening hours. The gusty winds, estimated to be in excess of 40 mph, led to dust storm conditions near the town of Maricopa during the early afternoon hours. Visibilities in dense blowing dust along State Route 347 led to an auto accident; fortunately no fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deep and strong upper level low pressure system developed over the desert southwest on October 9th and it led to the development of strong and gusty west winds over the central deserts during the afternoon and evening hours. Gusty winds estimated to be in excess of 40 mph generated dense blowing dust near the town of Maricopa, and dust storm conditions were reported by local law enforcement. At 1315 MST, they reported near zero visibility in dust storm conditions 5 miles north of Maricopa on State Route 347. An auto accident occurred as a result of the low visibility however no injuries or fatalities were reported. Additionally, at 1430 MST an NWS employee 8 miles north of Maricopa reported visibility down to near zero miles along State Route 347 from Riggs Road to the Gila River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud near Mormon Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud  about 6 miles east of Mormon Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain hit northern Navajo and northern Apache Counties. Flash flooding covered highways around Kayenta northward toward Monument Valley and near Round Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding on Highway 160 near mile post 408 and on Highway 163 near mile post 400.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain hit northern Navajo and northern Apache Counties. Flash flooding covered highways around Kayenta northward toward Monument Valley and near Round Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding over Highway 191 near mile post 497.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and street flooding in Taylor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding (curb to curb) in Taylor.  Water was also flowing between homes and creating trenches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure was located over central Nevada with a strong pressure gradient over Arizona. Strong southwest winds occurred ahead of the extremely dry cold front and produced areas of blowing dust. Visibilities near zero contributed to a fatal multi-vehicle accident in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Antecedent dry conditions along with gusty winds of about 20 to 25 mph produced localized dense blowing dust that reduced visibility to near zero in parts of Pinal County. This localized blowing dust resulted in a multiple vehicle chain-reaction accident along Interstate 10 near Picacho at mile marker 214. The accident involved 19 vehicles and resulted in 3 fatalities and 12 injuries.  The interstate was closed to traffic in both directions until around 10 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought strong winds to northern Arizona on September 26, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust measured at the Flagstaff Airport ASOS was 64 MPH at 312 PM. The winds gusted above 50 MPH for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the South Rim of the Grand Canyon produced heavy rain,  trail damage, and damage to buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced about one inch of rain in under an hour. This created some flooding problems in the Grand Canyon Village and below the Rim. One range guage in the area reported 0.92 inches of rain in 40 minutes. Another guage in the area reported 1.85 inches of rain between 135 PM and 245 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain street flooding in the Payson area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded some low water crossings in the Payson area. Radar estimated that 1.00 to 1.24 inches of rain fell between 145 and 230 PM MST.  A weather spotter reported 1.75 inches of rain during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over northern New Mexico steered drier air westward into southeast Arizona.  However, adequate moisture remained from Tucson westward to fuel thunderstorms. Thunderstorms caused wind damage and flash flooding in northwest sections of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped nearly an inch of rain in 20 minutes. The heavy rain caused drain spouts on the roof of a supermarket to break, sending water into the attic, which collapsed the ceiling. The store was evacuated but no one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over northern New Mexico steered drier air westward into southeast Arizona.  However, adequate moisture remained from Tucson westward to fuel thunderstorms. Thunderstorms caused wind damage and flash flooding in northwest sections of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain filled many normally dry washes in parts of northwest Tucson.  Orange Grove Road had 2 feet of water flowing across it near Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over northern New Mexico steered drier air westward into southeast Arizona.  However, adequate moisture remained from Tucson westward to fuel thunderstorms. Thunderstorms caused wind damage and flash flooding in northwest sections of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees on Tucson's northwest side.  In addition, an adobe chimney was toppled onto its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over northern New Mexico steered drier air westward into southeast Arizona.  However, adequate moisture remained from Tucson westward to fuel thunderstorms. Thunderstorms caused wind damage and flash flooding in northwest sections of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees on Tucson's northwest side. One tree branch damaged three cars when it fell. Additionally, a small greenhouse was damaged by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over northern New Mexico steered drier air westward into southeast Arizona.  However, adequate moisture remained from Tucson westward to fuel thunderstorms. Thunderstorms caused wind damage and flash flooding in northwest sections of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced gusty winds at Saguaro National Park East with a 63 mph gust measured near Old Spanish Trail and Freeman Road.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was centered over the central United States, with weak southwest flow over Arizona. The morning sounding was quite moist, with about 1.5 of precipitable water. A shallow layer of drier air intruded into the area with easterly surface winds. A few showers and thunderstorms formed, some of which produced strong outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow from a light rain shower collapsed part of a crudely supported porch attached to a house southwest of Pima, AZ. A nearby carport was undamaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the evening hours on September 17th. High temperatures that day were well in excess of 110 degrees and in fact an Excessive Heat Warning was in effect. Due to the extreme heat and relatively dry conditions, thunderstorms were able to generate very strong and damaging outflow winds which affected the greater Phoenix area, including the community of Buckeye. Gusty winds in excess of 40 mph moved through Buckeye during the evening, spreading dense blowing dust and causing a number of small trees and cactus to be completely blown over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the evening hours on September 17th. Due to the excessive heat and relatively dry conditions, the storms generated strong and gusty outflow winds, estimated to be in excess of 40 mph. The winds caused areas of blowing dust which affected the community of Buckeye. In addition to the blowing dust, the gusty winds were sufficient in strength to blow down a number of small trees. According to a trained weather spotter in Buckeye, evening thunderstorm winds blew down a number of small trees and large cactus, leaving the roots completely exposed. The damage was near the Sundance Towne Center and in the surrounding neighborhoods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water caused thunderstorms to dump heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud extended briefly from a thunderstorm southeast of Sonoita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture of between 1.5 and 2 inches of precipitable water and weak upper flow resulted in slow moving thunderstorms that produced heavy rain over western and central Pima County. Flash flooding occurred on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2 to 3 inches fell along the Hickiwan Wash between 2 and 4 pm and downstream along the San Simon Wash between the between 4 and 6 pm. The San Simon Wash flowed rapidly across Highway 86, and Indian Routes 28 and 21 downstream, causing all these roads to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture of between 1.5 and 2 inches of precipitable water and weak upper flow resulted in slow moving thunderstorms that produced heavy rain over western and central Pima County. Flash flooding occurred on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued overnight along the San Simon Wash from Santa Cruz downstream due to heavy rain that fell during the late afternoon hours. Indian Road 21 remained closed due to high water until early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prominent ridge was in place over the center United States, with very moist air in place over southern Arizona. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches.  Weak upper level flow caused storms to move slowly. Some storms produced 2 to 3 inches of rainfall, creating flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain in less than an hour caused flash flooding northwest of Willcox. The home of a family of four was flooded.  As they attempted to escape in their sport utility vehicle, water was nearly waste high.  They drove toward Willcox but became stranded in flood waters along Old Stewart Road and needed to be rescued. The next day, another vehicle became stranded in standing water in the same location. The man was able to swim to safety. No injuries were reported in either incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although cloud cover kept temperatures in SE Arizona several degrees cooler than normal, portions of Cochise County saw enough clearing in weak southwest flow for daytime surface heating to combine with high dewpoints to ignite storms which produced severe wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bisbee-Douglas Airport ASOS recorded thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the late afternoon hours on August 17th, and they affected the community of Roosevelt. The storms generated gusty outflow winds estimated to be at least 60 mph, and they prompted the issuance of a Severe Thunderstorm Warning. The strong winds caused damage to awnings and windows at an RV park in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of southern Gila county during the afternoon hours on August 17th, and they affected the community of Roosevelt. The storms generated gusty and damaging winds. A trained weather spotter in Roosevelt estimated a wind gust to 60 mph. He also reported additional storm damage to the Roosevelt Lakeview Park Mobile Home RV Park. The strong winds, estimated to be at least 60 mph, tore up awnings and broke windows at some of the properties at the RV park. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the Roosevelt area at the time; it was issued at 1703MST and ran until 1800MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on August 30th. The atmosphere was extremely moist and rather unstable and this allowed thunderstorms with strong gusty winds and heavy rain to develop. The storms primarily affected east and southeast valley communities such as Tempe, Chandler and Apache Junction. The stronger storms produced wet microbursts which downed a large number of trees in south Tempe around 5 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across the eastern portion of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on August 30th. The storms generated strong and gusty winds, estimated to be upwards of 60 mph. According to a report from local law enforcement, the gusty winds blew down power poles 1 mile west of Apache Junction, at the intersection of Meridian and Apache Trail. Although a Severe Thunderstorm Warning was not in effect, there was a Significant Weather Advisory issued for the Apache Junction area at 1710MST, indicating a potential for gusty winds in excess of 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county during the evening hours on August 31st and they affected communities such as Quartzsite. Rainfall rates based on radar reached as high as 2 inches per hour, and this was sufficient to cause flash flooding. The flooding caused area roads to become impassable, but no injuries were reported as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county during the evening hours on August 31st, and they affected the community of Quartzsite. Due to the very moist nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced locally heavy rain with rainfall rates in the neighborhood of 2 inches per hour, based on radar rainfall estimates. The heavy rain caused area washes, such as the Tyson and Plumosa washes, to run heavily and flash flooding occurred. According to a newpaper report, at 2300MST the intersection of South Riggles Avenue and Rodgers was impassable due to flash flooding. The intersection was about 1 mile east of Quartzsite and just south of Interstate 10. A Flash Flood Warning was issued at 2224MST for the community of Quartzsite and it remained in effect through 0015MST on September 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin for several days, peaking on the 25th. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning knocked out power to 186 customers in Bullhead City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boulders washed onto the road near the intersection of Alamo Road and Boriana Mine Road. Alamo Road south of Yucca was damaged by flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water ran over McCulloch Blvd. in Lake Havasu City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shear axis east of Tucson, between two upper level highs, combined with ample moisture to fire slow-moving, early morning storms in Southern AZ.  Impacts included thunderstorm wind damage and flash flooding in and near the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through southern Tucson, damaging parts of the roof of Van Buskirk Elementary School and downing several power poles in the area. Power poles were also blown down at the intersection of Irvington Rd. and Campbell Ave. A total of 9,000 customers were without power for a short period of time. Additionally, several trees in the area were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westward moving low pressure centered over Baja California helped to fuel storms over Southern Arizona.  Severe thunderstorms impacted southern sections of the Tucson metro area causing thunderstorm wind damage and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of around one inch in less than 30 minutes caused flash flooding across portions of the Tucson metro area.  Tucson���s southeast side was hit hard along Houghton Road from Sahuarita Road north to Golf Links Road then west to around Wilmot Road.  Motorists became stranded in vehicles and several people were trapped in washes when the normally dry stream beds began to flow rapidly. One person was injured after being washed downstream in Rose Hill Wash on Tucson���s east side. Other swift water rescues occurred in the Pantano Wash near Golf Links Road and the Rillito River near La Canada Drive. In addition, a motorist was rescued from his vehicle near Creek Street and Bonanza Avenue. The vehicle was later swept away. Near the Tucson International Airport, six inches of pavement depth along a section of South Sears Boulevard near the was completely washed away. Tucson���s southwest side also received heavy rain totaling 1 to 2 inches, especially along Valencia Road from Campbell Avenue westward to near Ryan Field.  Several cars were stranded along San Joaquin Road and sections of Snyder Hill Road and Old Ajo Highway were closed due to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of flowing water with rocks and debris covered the intersection of Chino Dr. and Teddy Roosevelt Rd. in Golden Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bullhead Parkway was closed due to mud, boulders, and debris in the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud flowed across the Oatman-Topock Highway at the Sacramento Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin for several days, peaking on the 25th. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of water flowed down Johnson Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere across south central Arizona was very moist and unstable on August 26th, due in part to lingering moisture that had spread in earlier from former tropical storm Ivo. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across much of south central Arizona during the middle of the afternoon, and they continued into the evening hours. The storms produced a wide variety of weather which affected much of Maricopa and northwest Pinal counties, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Flooding and flash flooding, strong and damaging thunderstorm outflow winds, dust storms and hail were all produced at one time or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 26th, thunderstorms developed well into the evening hours across far northwestern portions of Maricopa county and they affected the community of Wickenburg. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable; as a result the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain with rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. Locally heavy rain began to fall in the Wickenburg area after 1930MST and by 2026MST an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued. Thunderstorms with heavy rain continued well into the evening leading to flooding of area roads and washes. At 2240MST a trained spotter in Wickenburg reported that the Sols Wash at the unbridged crossing at Vulture Mine Road was flowing 2 feet deep. Despite the high flow, it was contained and not spilling out over the banks. In addition, Maricopa County Flood Control District gages indicated significant stream flow in the Flying E and Casandro Washes near Wickenburg; these washes are known to impact several roads in the area. Flash Flood Warnings were not issued for the area, however Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories did remain in effect until just past midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture in weak southeast flow resulted in the scattered slow moving thunderstorms across southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced locally heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Purdy Lane to become flooded in Naco. Flood waters entered one home near the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoonal high was positioned of the Central Plains allowing tropical moisture to advance northward into southeast Arizona from the remnants of tropical storm Ivo. Afternoon thunderstorms dropped heavy amounts of rain around the region, causing flash flooding of some roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wash running through Vail flooded near the intersection of Calle Rinconado and Andrada Road. Water flowing across the road was over one foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere led to the formation of widespread showers and thunderstorms across south central Arizona during the morning hours on July 21st. The storms were persistent and continued to develop and move slowly across the deserts well into the afternoon hours, leading to significant flooding and flash flooding. Due to the exeedingly moist nature of the airmass, there was little if any wind damage with the thunderstorms,  however very heavy rain leading to flooding was common; rainfall rates were as high as 3 inches per hour at times. Some locations that were hit especially hard by the flooding included Scottsdale and Apache Junction. A variety of hydrological products were issued on Sunday including Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories, Flash Flood Warnings and Flood Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 21st, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning hours across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, and they intensified into the early afternoon, producing locally heavy rain with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to widespread flooding of streets and washes across Apache Junction, which was hit harder by flooding than the surrounding communities. Area spotters reported rain totals between one and two inches within an hour due to the heavy rain. At 1212 pm, an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for Apache Junction and surrounding communities, and this advisory was upgraded to a Flash Flood Warning at 1252 pm. According to a trained weather spotter, at 119 pm flash flooding was occurring in the Weeks Wash in Apache Junction and water was flooding into surrounding residential yards. The flash flooding continued into the early afternoon and a 212 pm, local law enforcement reported that a water rescue took place in Weeks Wash. Apache Junction Firefighters, wading through thigh-high water, rescued 2 people whose pickup truck stalled in the middle of the wash according to Deputy Fire Chief Mike Farber. Additionally, two 19 year old men were trapped in a tree near Lost Dutchman State Park until they were rescued by an Arizona Department of Public Safety helicopter and the Pinal County Sheriff's  Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shear axis east of Tucson, between two upper level highs, combined with ample moisture to fire slow-moving, early morning storms in Southern AZ.  Impacts included thunderstorm wind damage and flash flooding in and near the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along Silverlake Road and Euclid Avenue near downtown Tucson. Vehicles became stalled in the floodwaters and the road was then closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure over southern California drew tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lorena into southern Arizona. Precipitable water values near 2 inches contributed to widespread thunderstorms from Tucson westward but isolated storms occurred to the east of Tucson, one of which produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed and photographed by a member of the media northeast of Mescal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level high pressure over New Mexico allowed for a southeast flow into southeast Arizona but upper level winds remained out of the southwest.  Thunderstorms formed over Cochise and Graham counties and moved northeast producing isolated areas of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused minor roof damage to several structures in Thatcher and Pima, and downed four power poles east of Fort Thomas. The wind knocked a travel trailer off its block, which in turn damaged a nearby cinder block wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture seeped northward into western portions of southern Arizona, leading to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the house of a trained spotter in Vail, resulting in electrical damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure retreated to the four corners region.  Hot daytime temperatures and modest southerly flow produced thunderstorms with gusty winds in Pima and Pinal Counties.  Winds created blowing dust that limited visibility to near zero along Interstate 10 near Eloy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucson International Airport ASOS recorded severe thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph.  An eight inch diameter tree branch was downed near the intersection of Sunrise and Skyline Drives.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure was centered over eastern Colorado, allowing adequate moisture to seep north into southern Arizona. Thunderstorms were widespread, but only a couple produced large hail and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail north of Glenn St. and Alvernon Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure was centered over eastern Colorado, allowing adequate moisture to seep north into southern Arizona. Thunderstorms were widespread, but only a couple produced large hail and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the roof of an apartment building near 29th Street and Craycroft Road. The lightning penetrated the roof's asphalt shingles and caused damage to the cinder block wall below the roof's eave. A nearby palm tree was also ignited by the strike. One resident was knocked over even though he was in a different room, but no one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure was centered over eastern Colorado, allowing adequate moisture to seep north into southern Arizona. Thunderstorms were widespread, but only a couple produced large hail and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 2 inches of rain in less than an hour led to flash flooding along Valencia Road in southwest Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere led to the formation of widespread showers and thunderstorms across south central Arizona during the morning hours on July 21st. The storms were persistent and continued to develop and move slowly across the deserts well into the afternoon hours, leading to significant flooding and flash flooding. Due to the exeedingly moist nature of the airmass, there was little if any wind damage with the thunderstorms,  however very heavy rain leading to flooding was common; rainfall rates were as high as 3 inches per hour at times. Some locations that were hit especially hard by the flooding included Scottsdale and Apache Junction. A variety of hydrological products were issued on Sunday including Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories, Flash Flood Warnings and Flood Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts during the morning hours on July 21st and they became more intense and numerous as the morning progressed. By late morning, thunderstorms were widespread across the northeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, including Scottsdale and Paradise Valley, and rain rates in excess of two inches per hour were indicated by rain gage reports and radar estimates. According to county officials, a Maricopa County Flood Control District rain gage on East Doubletree Ranch Road near North Scottsdale Road in central Scottsdale measured 1.65 inches of rain within 30 minutes ending at 1111 am. A Flood Control rain gage on Shea Boulevard near Highway 51 in Paradise Valley measured 1.97 inches of rain within 30 minutes ending at 1120 am. Also, a flood control gage in Cave Creek measured 1.73 inches of rain in one hour, ending at 1144 am. The heavy rain led to multiple road closures across northeast parts of the greater Phoenix area including Scottsdale and it led to multiple swift water rescues. Although Flash Flood Warnings were not issued for Scottsdale, Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued beginning at about 10 am and continuing through 130 pm. Another Flood Advisory was later issued for Scottsdale at 312 pm which continued through 5 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water caused thunderstorms to dump heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded Houghton and Wilmot Roads between Tucson and Corona De Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water caused thunderstorms to dump heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sabino Creek rose 1 to 2 feet as it flowed through Sabino Canyon Recreation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water caused thunderstorms to dump heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on and downstream from Sierrita Road caused flooding of washes flowing northwest toward Three Points. Sierrita Mountain Road was flooded near Guy St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with abundant moisture of nearly 2 inches of precipitable water caused thunderstorms to dump heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding on the west side of Tucson.  Four people were rescued from a vehicle in 2 to 3 feet of water flowing across Manville Road near Anway Road in Picture Rocks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure over southern California drew tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lorena into southern Arizona on September 9th.  Precipitable water values near 2 inches contributed to widespread thunderstorms from Tucson westward.  Heavy rain occurred late on the 9th which led to flooding of Brawley Wash on September 10th in southwest Tuscon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours of September 9th, three to four inches of rain fell along the east slopes of the Baboquivari Mountains and northern portions of the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge along a 10 mile length of the Altar Wash 20 to 30 miles south of Three Points, Arizona.  The flow peaked downstream at Three Points along the Brawley Wash at about 7500 cubic feet per second around 5:00 am MST on September 10th. While this represented a rise of more than six feet, only a small portion of this flow  poured over the banks downstream of this gauge, flooding a sub-division with 8 to 12 inches of water.  Roads were eventually closed although first responders were able to evacuate residents from ten mobile homes.  The residents were unable to return until the following day.  Minor damage occurred to several vehicles and mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prominent ridge was in place over the center United States, with very moist air in place over southern Arizona. Precipitable water values were near 2 inches.  Weak upper level flow caused storms to move slowly. Some storms produced 2 to 3 inches of rainfall, creating flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain caused mud to slide downhill, temporarily closing Highway 191 in Clifton near the old train depot. Debris was up to four feet deep across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced scattered heavy rain across northern Arizona. Some storms lasted through the night. Some locations (Cottonwood, Sedona, Heber-Overgaard, and Parks) received an inch to an inch and a half in 24 hours without any flooding issues. Other locations had flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused at least four inches of rain along Highway 89 from Fanning to Townsend-Winona near the Flagstaff Mall. Water was running in all normally dry washes from McCann Estates to Campbell Road below the Radio Fire (1977) and the Schultz Fire (2010).  A few roads in that area had minor amounts of water flowing over them. Soliere was flooded between Country Club Drive and 4th Street and was closed. Later in the evening (753-808 PM), 1/3 of an inch fell in just 15 minutes in Kachina Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in Antelope Canyon south east of Page.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Antelope Canyon southeast of Page. Eight to 12 inches of new sand was deposited in this popular slot canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding north of Wickenburg in Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Rincon Road were closed along Martinez Wash due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding north of St Johns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Highway 191 between mile markers 322 and 324.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and flash flooding along Indian Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm produced flash flooding along Indian Creek in Indian Gardens. Minor damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere led to the formation of widespread showers and thunderstorms across south central Arizona during the morning hours on July 21st. The storms were persistent and continued to develop and move slowly across the deserts well into the afternoon hours, leading to significant flooding and flash flooding. Due to the exeedingly moist nature of the airmass, there was little if any wind damage with the thunderstorms,  however very heavy rain leading to flooding was common; rainfall rates were as high as 3 inches per hour at times. Some locations that were hit especially hard by the flooding included Scottsdale and Apache Junction. A variety of hydrological products were issued on Sunday including Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories, Flash Flood Warnings and Flood Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts during the morning hours on July 21st and they became more intense and numerous as the morning progressed. By late morning, thunderstorms were widespread across the northeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, including Scottsdale, and rain rates between one and two inches per hour were indicated by rain gage reports and radar estimates. An Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was initially issued for the Scottsdale area beginning around 11 am and it continued until 130 pm. The heavy rains led to significant flooding and flash flooding across Scottsdale, numerous roads were inundated and closed and area washes, including the Indian Bend Wash, ran heavily. According to an article posted on-line at azcentral.com, multiple swift water rescues were needed in north Scottsdale. In one rescue, crews extricated two people whose vehicle became stuck near Scottsdale Road and Sutton Drive. In a much more significant rescue, Scottsdale crews utilized rescue swimmers to save a stranded motorist near 83rd Street and Desert Cove Avenue. More than 25 firefighters from Tempe, Phoenix and Scottsdale responded to the scene according to Scottsdale battalion chief David Folio. Additionally, a Paradise Valley woman was rescued after her vehicle was stuck near Tatum Boulevard and Desert Park Place according to Phoenix Fire Captain Larry Nunez. Times for the rescues were unspecified in the articles, but likely occurred sometime from late morning into early afternoon. Although Flash Flood Warnings were not issued for Scottsdale during this event, another Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was later issued at 312 pm and it continued until 5 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms developed across the central Arizona deserts during the morning hours on July 21st, and they affected portions of southwest Maricopa county extending from Gila Bend eastward into Pinal county. The storms persisted into the late morning and they generated locally heavy rains with rainfall rates between one and two inches per hour. For exmaple, at 714 am a trained spotter 6 miles west of Stanfield reported 0.58 inches of rain fell in just 10 minutes, causing streams in the area to run heavily. An Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for southwest Maricopa county at 655 am, continuing until 845 am. At 950 am, local law enforcement officials reported that State Route 283 between the town of Maricopa and Gila Bend had been barricaded by sheriff's deputies due to flash flooding. The heavy rains over central Arizona started around 6 am, and since the flooding occurred less than 6 hours later, it is considered to be flash flooding. No Flash Flood Warnings were in effect at the time of the flooding over State Route 238.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture fueled widespread thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud and gravel flowed over Stockton Hill Road at Northern Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of flowing water with large rocks and debris covered streets in Lake Havasu City. A spotter measured 0.66 inch of rain in 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced scattered heavy rain across northern Arizona. Some storms lasted through the night. Some locations (Cottonwood, Sedona, Heber-Overgaard, and Parks) received an inch to an inch and a half in 24 hours without any flooding issues. Other locations had flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bright Angel Creek near Phantom Ranch rose 2 feet in a flash flood late in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure centered just north of the four corners region along with low pressure located over the southern Nevada helped pull monsoonal moisture northward from Mexico into southern Arizona, fueling storms with damaging thunderstorm winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured a thunderstorm wind gust to 58 mph northwest of Hereford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure centered just north of the four corners region along with low pressure located over the southern Nevada helped pull monsoonal moisture northward from Mexico into southern Arizona, fueling storms with damaging thunderstorm winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter west of Hereford reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system drifted north out of Sonora into southern Arizona during the early morning, bringing with it showers and thunderstorms.  Moisture rich storms  caused flash flooding in Cochise County during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain fell in less than 30 minutes on the southwest slopes of the Mule Mountains and in Old Bisbee causing extensive street flooding of the historic district. A retaining wall collapsed due to the force of the flowing water. The wall fell on a vehicle, creating dents and breaking the windshield. Flooding also left debris covering Highway 92 near the San Pedro River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts during the afternoon hours on September 6th and they affected portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The atmosphere was dry in the low levels, unstable, and very hot. Afternoon high temperatures in the central deserts reached or exceeded 110 degrees in the hottest locales. The combination of those conditions allowed the thunderstorms to generate strong and damaging winds, with peak gusts in excess of 50 mph. The strong winds were sufficient to knock down trees in central Phoenix. In addition, the gusty winds picked up significant amounts of dust and created dust storm conditions. Blowing Dust Advisories and Dust Storm Warnings were both issued for the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated afternoon thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area on September 6th. High temperatures reached to around 110 degrees and due to the very hot and somewhat dry nature of the atmosphere, the storms were able to generate strong and gusty outflow winds. The peak wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph or more. strong wind gusts blew down a 1 foot diameter tree on top of a carport approximately 2 miles northwest of Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport, as shown by local broadcast media footage. In addition, a member of the public reported that an 8 inch diameter Palo Verde tree was uprooted. The tree was located 2 miles northeast of the Arizona State Fairgrounds. Finally, a tweet received from the public showed a picture of a tree downed at the intersection of 24th Street and the 202. The tweet also mentioned that a number of trees were downed in the area.  A Severe Thunderstorm Warning had been issued for the affected areas beginning at 1716MST and continuing until 1815MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three swift water rescues occurred in Golden Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: DW Ranch Road and Blake Ranch Road were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and a moderate easterly mid level flow produced thunderstorms with damaging winds, large hail and heavy rain across parts of southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Elfrida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow spread copious amounts of tropical moisture northward and into the south central Arizona deserts during the morning hours on September 9th. Widespread showers with moderate to heavy rain developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and this led to episodes of flash flooding and urban flooding which started in the late morning hours. In addition to the showers, there were embedded thunderstorms which served to locally enhance rainfall totals. Local storm spotters along with Flood Control District gage reports indicated that between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell during the day. Numerous reports of flash flooding and urban flooding were received from across the greater Phoenix area, starting in the late morning and continuing into the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow imported copious amounts of tropical moisture into south central Arizona during the morning hours on September 9th, and as a result widespread showers developed across the southeastern portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Areas of moderate to heavy rain developed, isolated embedded thunderstorms further enhanced the rainfall totals. Flood Control District gages and radar data indicated that rainfall rates up to two inches per hour had occurred during the late morning and this led to flash flooding between Sun Lakes and San Tan Valley. According to broadcast media reports, at approximately 1200MST there was flooding near the intersection of Riggs and Power Roads. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1109MST and it remained in effect through 1230MST. Additional rains continued in the area into the afternoon hours, leading to the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a northeast upper level flow continued, low level moisture increased aboard lower level southeast winds. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding in parts of the Tucson metro area and locations just south and east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed two dozen power poles in southern Tucson, mainly along Valencia Road between Park and Campbell Avenues and near the intersection of Golf Links and Pantano Roads. Around 8,000 customers were without electrical power at one point. Thirty mobile homes were damaged by winds or downed trees near Benson Highway and Valencia Road. One tree fell on a car. In addition, two hangar doors were blown off and two trees were downed at the Tucson International Airport where a wind gust to 74 mph was measured at 205 p.m. MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the South Rim of the Grand Canyon produced heavy rain,  trail damage, and damage to buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding and trail damage on the Bright Angel Trail at Garden Creek below Indian Gardens.  Flooding covered an intersection at the South Rim by one foot of mud. The basement of the Park Headquarters flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin for several days, peaking on the 25th. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Route 1 was closed from Highway 95 to Route 66 due to water, rocks, and debris over the road in low lying areas. One swift water rescue was conducted two miles north of Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture fueled widespread thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in west and northeast Flagstaff. One inch diameter hail was also reported in east Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flooding on roads in east Flagstaff. Some roads became impassable...including historic Route 66 for about 20 minutes. Flood waters entered 15 to 20 homes. About 2.25 inches of rain fell between 11 AM and 1 PM on Fanning Drive below the Pipeline Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of far southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours on August 22nd, and they affected the community of Yuma, as well as surrounding communities such as Fortuna Foothills and Somerton. The thunderstorms were quite intense and produced a wide variety of weather, including strong damaging winds in excess of 50 mph, locally dense blowing dust, moderate hail and locally heavy rain. Winds were sufficient to blow down power lines and blow over trailer trucks. Hail nearly one inch in diameter fell in Yuma, and rainfall in the Fortuna Foothills approached 2 inches, leading to localized flooding. A wide variety of products were issued including Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the Yuma area during the afternoon hours on August 22nd, and they produced a variety of weather elements including moderate hail. Hail measured at 0.88 inches in diameter was reported in Yuma. The report was received via amateur radio. The report also indicated that visibility was less than one quarter of a mile in blowing dust. The storms led to the issuance of a Significant Weather Advisory for Yuma beginning at 1700MST and continuing until 1800MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across the Navajo Nation. Several communities were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Close to 50 families were displace across the Navajo Nation in Apache County because of the flash flooding. Part of the Navajo Zoo was inundated with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rain over portions of Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 1.28 inches of rain fell in just 45 minutes ten miles south of Mayer.
